# Changelog

## [Unreleased]

### Changed

- **New standing principle: Parallel by Default; Serial Requires Evidence (#757 / #760).** Concurrency is the default for any frontier; holding work serial must cite present-tense, checkable evidence for a named serializer — S1 data dependency (name the concrete artifact one unit consumes from another), S2 shared irreversible effect (name the shared resource), S3 environment (a failed worktree-support probe). Uncertainty is not a serializer, and guesses/anticipations are inadmissible grounds for serial: wrong-parallel costs one bounded synthesis pass inside isolated worktrees, wrong-serial costs invisible wall-clock on every frontier. The principle governs mode only (width stays sized by faithful decomposition) and its recorded evidence line is audit-only. Stated in the README Philosophy and CLAUDE.md Workflow Design Principles now; the mechanical inversion in the executor ships with the progressive-elaboration epic. Propagated 2026-07-22 to the agent-facing surfaces: `templates/axioms.md` gains a **Parallel by default** paragraph mirrored byte-identically into its six workflow-init embeds; the planner profile (`agents/workflow-planner.md` + the three byte-matched tomls) gains the evidence-backed `sequence` rule; the plan-run skeleton gains a **Serial requires evidence** block, regenerated across its twelve surfaces. Byte-identity and toml-parity guards verified standalone at commit time; the four chains were deliberately deferred (planning session) — run them before the next release cut.
- **Spine plan grammar: freeze a coarse milestone spine with typed expansion points — #758 (epic #757 child 1).** The plan-validator gains a second plan form alongside the full DAG: an ordered milestone spine with a unique sink, where a node is either a concrete single-role node or an `expansion_point` whose frontier is composed at open time rather than proven at freeze. Spine-level proofs stay global — unique sink, sink post-dominance on the spine, the reviewed-before-sink wall, closed library, caps, and governance all still apply — but the interior whole-plan post-dominance and antichain-disjointness proofs are NOT applied to an expansion point, because its members do not exist yet at freeze. Each expansion point declares its milestone goal, coarse advisory expected surfaces, join constraints, and review-obligation class; `plan_hash` covers the spine only. Migration is dual-accept: a legacy full-DAG plan freezes and runs byte-identically, pinned by regression. A pure-spine plan is held to the same `validation_policy_required` fail-closed rule as a DAG plan — an expansion point counts as a code producer because its open-time frontier may compose any writer role and freeze cannot prove otherwise, so a spine plan with no validation policy refuses exactly as the byte-equivalent DAG plan does. Adversarially verified and closed 2026-07-22; all four edition chains green with captured per-chain exit codes. Known seam handed forward by design: the review-journal binding moves to `(spine plan_hash, expansion record id)` in the expansion-transaction child (#759), and `expansion_id` becomes a validated review-journal attempt field in the local-re-expansion child (#761) — the first consumer that needs to ask which record an attempt reviewed.

### Added

- **Expansion transaction: compose an expansion point's frontier at open time, review-bound and crash-repairable — #759 (epic #757 child).** An `expansion_point` node now opens through `expand-open` (reserves the composition, writes the expansion record + its members into the `## Node Ledger`) and settles through `expand-close`, so a spine node's real writer/reviewer frontier materializes only when reached. The review-journal attempt key becomes `(spine plan_hash, expansion record id)` — pinned by `expansionIdFieldOk`/`EXPANSION_ID_RE` in `adaptive-schema.js` — so a review always names which record it certified. `rollForwardExpansions` repairs a crash in all three windows (not-yet-opening, opening-not-composed, composed-not-closed) rather than leaving a half-open expansion, and a composed gate-role unit is reserved at compose time so it opens and closes under the same schema view (the first pass's `review_context_mismatch` wedge was fixed before accept). Adversarially verified; four edition chains green. Descopes carried to named owners: `expansion_id` review-attempt validation → #761, composed gate units invisible to `--gate-verify`/`--verdict-check` → #762, `workflow-tasks.json` mirror omitting composed units → #763, the six workflow-init surfaces → #765.
- **Local re-expansion: a finding owned by an already-discharged writer re-opens its own expansion point instead of forcing a full replan — #761 (epic #757 child).** When review surfaces a defect whose owner is a writer inside a closed expansion (not the tail), `routeFindingReExpansion` + `resolveExpansionOwnership`/`surfaceCoversFile` locate the owning expansion point and `reexpand-open` (`runReExpandOpen`) re-composes just that frontier — reviewed before it can retire via `assertReExpansionReviewed`, discharged-point re-open cascade wired at `runRepairNodeCore`/`runReopenNode`/`prepareReplanUnlocked`. `spineReExpansionFirst` makes `replan.js` refuse `replan_superseded_by_local_reexpansion` when a local re-expansion can own the finding, so the heavier replan lane is reserved for defects no single expansion can contain. `expansion_id` is now an optional-validated review-journal attempt field. Adversarially verified (M10 kill); four edition chains green. Carry-forward: re-expansion wiring integration coverage (M7b) → #762.
- **Serialization inversion: the executor defaults every frontier to co-open and requires named evidence to hold serial — #760 mechanism (epic #757 child).** The mechanical counterpart to the standing principle above: `adaptive-schema.js` and the routing skeletons/slots now treat parallel as the default and a `sequence` edge as a positive claim carrying an S1/S2/S3 serializer, with the evidence line recorded audit-only. Regenerated across the twelve routing surfaces; `adaptive-schema.js` byte-identical across all four editions. Four edition chains green.
- **Declared-not-walled write surfaces: an attributable out-of-surface companion file is handled by attribution + re-review, not a hard barrier refusal — #762 (epic #757 child).** A frozen expansion surface is now a DECLARATION for attribution and review-scoping rather than a hard wall. `amend-surface --node-id <point> --files "a,b"` (`runAmendSurface`) appends an append-only `amend(<point>):` block to `## Expansion Records` (outside the plan_hash body, so the frozen spine identity is untouched) attributing EXACT files — a directory-shaped or glob token is refused `amend_surface_not_exact_file` and dropped by the reader (`parseSurfaceAmendments`), the whole granularity contract — then routes the widened surface to RE-REVIEW through #761's `reexpand-open` (re-opens the owning point + its post-dominating review wall + resets the sink, and selectively re-verifies only the downstream milestones whose declared+amended surface intersects the fix). The whole-plan barrier (`barrierCheck`) unions an amended file into the declared allowlist ONLY when its owning point is `complete` (its re-review ran to completion), so a passed-review run that wedged at finalize on an out-of-surface required file (the #756 dead-ends 1/2/5) now clears — while an UNAMENDED out-of-surface write stays a fail-closed tamper refusal (`write_set_overflow` → the `unattributed_write` floor), the `foreign_archive`/`sensitive_write_unreviewed` precedence families stay intact, and a consent-gated (S2) surface still halts at the consent valve (amendment loosens attribution only, never consent). The durable amendments thread into `resolveExpansionOwnership`/`spineReExpansionFirst` via `mergedAmendedSurfaces`, so a finding on an already-amended companion routes LOCAL to its owning milestone (closing #761's M7b re-expansion-wiring integration coverage). Adversarially verified with mutation testing (13/13 self + 11/13 independent, the two survivors a test-coverage gap and an equivalent mutant, both non-blocking); walkthrough green. Cross-edition diff — `plan-validator.js`/`adaptive-node.js` are forge-ported aggregators (the gitlab/gitea/codex ports regenerate via `edition-sync`), so all four chains are required and were run green at merge (`adaptive-schema.js` itself untouched). Carry-forward explicitly refused: widening `--gate-verify`/`--verdict-check` to see composed gate units (the corrected #759 descope) is a real G1 semantic change (a reviewer must post-dominate each composed WRITER unit) and is NOT in scope here — composed gate units remain refused at compose time (`expansion_unit_role_gate_unsupported`); owner = the composed-gate review-context-seam workstream (the seam that refusal names).
- **Shared context packet + per-expansion efficiency evidence line — #763 (epic #757 child).** Two lightweight observability mechanisms, deliberately not a subsystem. Orientation emits `.cache/context-packet.md` (goal, constraints, key files, conventions, join expectations) and `readContextPacket` relays it verbatim into every real role dispatch card (open-next, open-ready, fused-advance) via conditional attach — lowering per-agent setup cost so the grain rule permits wider profitable fan-out. On expansion close, one evidence line (`expansion <id>: width=<N> mode=<serial|co_open> serializer=<none|S1|S2|S3> rework=<K>`) plus one archive rollup, pinned at both real archiver call sites, makes the fan-out turning point empirical. No token metering, timing infra, dashboards, or new durable files beyond the packet. Adversarially verified (13/14 mutants; delivery pinned by 6, archive pin by M18/M19); four edition chains green on the merged tree. Non-blocking carry-forward: fused-advance dispatch-site coverage (MPfused) → folded into the #756 acceptance harness (shipped code verified correct; only a direct dispatch-card assertion is owed).

### Fixed

- **A re-opened review wall on a passed-review run escalates to the sanctioned epoch transition instead of wedging — #756 (epic #757 child).** Closes the gap #762 handed forward: after `amend-surface` attributes an out-of-surface companion file (#762, clearing finalize dead-ends 1/2/5 at the barrier) and `reexpand-open` re-opens the owning milestone + its review wall (#761, clearing dead-ends 3/4/6 at the re-open), completing the wall's in-plan re-review still wedged `review_repair_delta_unavailable` — a settled-PASS change gate carries no fold boundary, so `deriveRepairDelta` cannot synthesize an in-plan repair delta, and the review-journal identity scheme (`attempt_id = <gate-node>:<ordinal>`, unique per journal + per-gate ordinal contiguity, all bound to one `plan_hash`) forbids a fresh in-journal discovery for the same gate node. The recovered file was ATTRIBUTED but never REVIEWED. Verified against the two independent designs + adversarial critic verdict — re-confirmed after #761 shipped, since the re-expansion record lives OUTSIDE the `plan_hash` body and does not relax the ordinal/attempt-id/binding invariants — **in-plan re-review of a passed wall is impossible, and the epoch transition is the only sanctioned exit** (`readSourceAuthority` Case B `diagnosis_to_build` already proves a replan ticket need not be a failed-review attempt). `prepareReviewOpen` now routes the settled-PASS-under-durable-re-expansion refusal to a typed `review_reexpansion_requires_epoch_transition` (naming the `diagnosis_to_build` route) instead of the bare dead end, via the new pure predicate `reexpansionBlocksInPlanReReview` — TRUE only when a settled PASS meets DURABLE re-expansion evidence (a `## Expansion Records` surface amendment or a record ordinal ≥ 2) on the wall's OWN owning expansion point (`wallOwningExpansionPoints`), derived solely from the frozen plan bytes and minting no synthetic scope id (the #722 cross-epoch phantom the critic flagged). A settled pass with no re-expansion, a settled FAIL (the ordinary in-plan repair-delta boundary), and a PASS carrying a valid fold marker are all untouched. The acceptance harness drives the full repro through the production CLIs (barrier + amend + re-open + re-discharge → the typed escalation) and proves the recovered file ends REVIEWED once carried through the epoch transition: a fresh-epoch `diagnosis_to_build` child re-reviews it as a clean discovery, producing a settled-PASS verdict whose reviewed candidate ENUMERATES the file, and the produced journal validates. Folds in the #763 carry-forward (MPfused): a pin that the fused-advance (`close-and-open-next`) dispatch card attaches `context_packet`. `deriveRepairDelta` itself is unchanged (`adaptive-schema.js` untouched); `adaptive-node.js` is a forge-ported aggregator, so the gitlab/gitea/codex ports regenerate via `edition-sync` and all four chains are required. 14 of 14 mutants killed on the new delta-boundary / recovery predicates; walkthrough green.
- **A replan source with an empty canonical frontier is refused — #729 (partial).** A settled failed-review attempt carrying `findings: []` was copied into the planner packet unconditionally and could activate a child epoch with no write node able to repair the defect that triggered the replan — turning one malformed receipt into a cross-epoch rediscovery loop (review fails → packet says `findings=[]` → child has no fixer → review finds the same bug again). Reproduced independently of the receipt-side fix, and **not merely a legacy concern**: a schema-1 `verdict: fail` + `findings_blocking: 0` receipt with no finding row yields a legal empty frontier, and the schema-2 `adversarial-verifier` `domain_outcome: refuted` shape is legal too because the coherence pair is explicitly gated off for that role. New pure predicate `reviewSourceCarriesFrontier` plus typed refusal `replan_source_findings_missing`, bound at every reachable frontier (source read, authority verification, planner packet, stored-transaction entry at both phases, and candidate re-author). **Two load-bearing scoping calls:** the guard is scoped to `authority_kind: 'review_outcome'`, because `diagnosis_to_build` legitimately carries `findings: []` — its evidence is four sealed artifacts, and un-scoping it would hard-block that lane (pinned by a killed mutant); and the predicate is *non-empty `findings`*, NOT *non-empty OPEN frontier*, because an attempt failing only on progress legitimately carries an all-`resolved` frontier whose record of what failed is intact — requiring an open UID would have false-refused a legal transition. 8 of 8 mutants killed across two rounds; test diff is 490 insertions, 0 deletions. **Semantic coverage (a child with findings but no node able to repair them) is deliberately NOT implemented**, with the soundness argument recorded inline rather than a guess: every mechanical proxy available at this seam is unsound — a finding's anchor records where a defect was OBSERVED and may legally carry no path at all, `fix_role` is advisory routing rather than a contract, and "child has any writer" does not even catch the live repro. A sound check needs the child plan to DECLARE which inherited finding UIDs each writer owns, which is a plan-grammar addition. **All 9 acceptance criteria now shipped (merged 2026-07-23).** The remaining 5 landed the plan-grammar addition this entry predicted: the planner declares `finding_owners` in the child `## Meta`, and a new `childFindingCoverage` wall refuses `replan_child_finding_owners_invalid` / `replan_child_finding_uncovered` unless every inherited finding that requires repair maps to a write-capable child node that reaches the designated certifier — an absent declaration never discharges an inherited finding, `none` is the only positive empty statement (a blank value refuses), and a duplicate `finding_owners:` line refuses rather than silently reassigning the finding to the last writer named. AC2 (the finding frontier carried into the planner packet under `packet.source.finding_index`) shipped separately. The predicate is the third finding-predicate in the codebase and is deliberately distinct from the two in `adaptive-schema.js`: it fails CLOSED (absence is the corruption signature it exists to catch), where the gate predicate `unresolvedInScopeFixes` fails open so omission cannot manufacture a blocker. Adversarially verified: all 9 ACs re-derived from the issue and driven through real `prepare`/`resume` transactions, 14 of 21 mutants killed, no blocking findings; the surviving mutants are defence-in-depth unreachable through the canonical lane (each independently refused by the validator's G1 rule before the coverage wall runs), except one — the two placements of the coverage wall (pre-freeze and inside `validateChildPlan`) are not distinguishable by outcome because the freeze writes the child bytes verbatim, so removing one copy is invisible to the suite; that gap is documented in the test rather than papered over with a fake pin.
- **Unified repair feedback delivery: a replan-child-reopened writer now receives the same digest-bound canonical brief as a direct repair — #730 (AC-9, final; issue complete).** The canonical, digest-bound finding brief (`buildRepairBrief`, whose assigned set is the fail-CLOSED `repairResponsibleFindings`) was seeded on the DIRECT repair-node path only; a writer reopened by a replan CHILD received the inherited frontier without it and had to reconstruct intent from the reviewer evidence named in `.cache/replan-source.json`. `kaola-workflow-replan.js` now mirrors the identical brief into the replan-child feedback path (`replanWriterBriefs` in `prepareReplanUnlocked`), so both reopen lanes deliver a byte-identical contract for the same attempt (proven by a real `prepare`→`resume` byte-equality test). The ownership partition deliberately reuses `repairResponsibleFindings` and NOT the gate predicate `unresolvedInScopeFixes`: repair asks "is this WRITER responsible?" and must fail CLOSED (a schema-1 row omitting `scope`/`action` is INCLUDED), where the gate asks "is there an unresolved in-scope fix?" and must fail OPEN so omission cannot manufacture a blocker — reverting to the gate predicate reds the suite. The prior scope-limit note in `docs/plan-run-cards/repair-routing.md` is removed. Adversarially verified; four edition chains green (cross-edition: `replan.js` is a forge-ported aggregator).

- **The `domain_outcome` enum is named at both ends of the reviewer contract — #727.** Open-time evidence seeding wrote every required review token as a generic `paste ... here` stub, and the close-time `review_domain_outcome_invalid` refusal had no operator-hint entry, so neither end named the valid values — which are role-kind-dependent (`approved | changes_requested` for approval gates, `refuted | not_refuted | indeterminate` for `adversarial-verifier`). A dispatcher that persisted a plausible-but-wrong token — `pass` / `fail`, exactly the strings `deriveGateEffect` emits as gate *effects* — discovered the mistake only at close and had to re-derive the enum from reviewer-contract docs, costing a re-persist round trip per miss. Both ends now name the allowed values for the gate's role kind, and `allowedDomainOutcomes` returns the schema's frozen arrays **by identity** (asserted by test) with the validator's inline ternary replaced by that same selector, so validator, seed and hint cannot drift. No refusal path was touched and the stub change is provably inert to the hollow-seed guards.

- **A repair-replan's child epoch can begin its first review gate — #722.** Replan activation deliberately preserves `.cache/review-attempts.json` (it is absent from `cleanupAllowed`, because cross-epoch history is indexed by scope lineage), so after a committed repair→replan the ACTIVE journal remains bound to the PARENT `plan_hash`. `prepareReviewOpen` read those raw bytes itself and validated them against the CHILD plan hash, bypassing the one blessed cross-epoch transformation (`readReviewJournal` → `loadCommittedLegacyImport` → `attachLegacyImport`) that the close path already used. Net effect: a schema-2 repair-replan child epoch could never begin its first review gate — the schema-2 repair-replan path had never run end-to-end. Reproduced through the real transaction path, which also surfaced a **second, previously unfiled instance of the same wedge**: a schema-1 parent journal refused `review_journal_version_mismatch` at the same seam. The fix is on the reader side and does not weaken the deliberate preservation: `readReviewLineageV2` becomes PURE over an already-resolved journal, and `prepareReviewOpen` resolves through `readReviewJournal` — the same reader, in the same order, as the close — then mirrors the close path's lane routing verbatim. A schema-2 parent now mints a schema-2 child via `buildCrossEpochChildJournal` instead of silently downgrading the whole child epoch to the schema-1 lane, which would have discarded the V2 review-context/candidate-partition binding the child plan's own contract requires. The schema-2 branch of `readReviewJournal` now resolves and fail-closes on `legacy_import` exactly like the schema-1 branch, so the persisted child journal keeps its digest-bound parent history on reread while a forged or dropped pointer still refuses. **Audit history is preserved and no integrity check was weakened:** the rotation applies only behind a committed, digest-verified replan transaction plus its epoch snapshot; absent one, the raw journal is still validated against the active plan and a `plan_hash` mismatch still refuses. Pinned end-to-end through the real transaction and node lifecycle over BOTH parent-journal schemas (test-replan 989 assertions, +45); a tampered pointer, a dropped pointer, and a declared import with no committed transaction all still fail closed. The open frontier now inherits refusal reasons the close already imposed — tighten-only, moving an existing wedge earlier, never permitting something previously refused.

- **A consented abandon evaluates the whole authority ladder before downgrading — #755.** `verifyCurrentEpochAuthority` returned on its FIRST failing tier, and all four run-state-progress reasons the abandon path is willing to downgrade sit ahead of the tiers it is not. So `archiveProjectDir` tested downgradability against whichever reason happened to be checked first and could not distinguish "the only faults are downgradable" from "a downgradable fault masked something worse". Compounding it, `verifyArchiveEpochAuthority` gated the entire snapshot tier on `current.ok`, so a deferrable first failure skipped snapshot verification altogether — staging residue, an unreadable or relocated `.cache/epochs`, and digest mismatches all went unexamined. Reproduced with paired probes: staging residue ALONE refuses correctly, while residue plus a missing compliance section archives the folder, removes the worktree and deletes the branch. `deferReasons` is now an opt-in caller policy naming exactly the reasons that caller will handle: those tiers record their failure and evaluation continues, every non-deferrable tier still returns immediately, and the verdict carries `deferrable_only` plus the complete `deferred_failures` list. Only the four progress tiers are deferrable — nothing later in the ladder reads them. A caller passing no policy is byte-identical, so no existing behavior moves. The downgrade decision now keys on the whole-ladder `deferrable_only` verdict rather than first-failure reason membership, and a deferred ledger tier skips the two progress checks that project the ledger map rather than evaluating them against garbage. The automatic PR-CLOSED sweep — the second production caller, driven by remote state rather than an operator's discard — now discloses `authority_downgraded` instead of leaving the downgrade observable only by diffing the archived folder. Hand-ported to the gitlab/gitea divergent `claim.js`/`replan.js` copies, which `edition-sync --write` does not touch and which both sync checkers report green without. Mutation-proven: restoring the `current.ok` snapshot gating kills six assertions, including both over-refusal controls.

- **Epoch snapshot verification is content-addressed, so a git round-trip no longer wedges an epoch forever — #754.** `verifySnapshotManifest` compared each file's recorded creation mode against `stat.mode & 0o777`, but replan writes some snapshot files `0600` and git preserves no mode bit except the executable one. Measured on the archived multi-epoch artifact: epoch 1 carried 1 mismatch and epoch 2 carried 3, all `want=600 got=644`, with EVERY content digest matching — so any snapshot that passed through an archive commit, a clone, or a fresh worktree checkout failed permanently and unrecoverably. The mode comparison is removed rather than narrowed to the executable bit: mode is not integrity evidence for a snapshot whose authority already rests on size, per-file SHA-256, and the authority projection recomputed from the archived plan BYTES. The manifest still RECORDS each file's creation mode as forensic metadata; verification simply does not compare it. The content digest is untouched — it is the load-bearing check, and it was deliberately not weakened to make the fixtures pass. Higher impact since the lineage work added a fourth caller of the same walk.

- **A settled failed schema-2 review no longer strands the run behind a stale task mirror — #733.** When a failed review folded its gate rows back to `pending`, the settlement wrote the plan ledger but never refreshed the generated `workflow-tasks.json`, and `verifyCurrentEpochAuthority` then refused `state_task_mirror_mismatch` on the next guarded lifecycle command. **The deadlock was total:** `orient` is the designated mirror reconciler, but the replan fence prologue runs `verifyCurrentEpochAuthority` and returns *before* `runOrient` is entered — so the one command that regenerates the mirror was blocked by a check on the mirror it would have fixed, leaving a hand-run of the task-mirror CLI as the only exit. Reproduced end-to-end through the real CLI on a committed epoch, with `orient`, `open-next` and `reconcile-running-set` all refusing. **The comparison is removed rather than synchronized**, because `workflow-tasks.json` is a pure projection of the plan: `generateMirror` takes only the plan content and derives every field from `readStoredHash` + `parseNodes` + `parseLedger`, so the deleted block compared re-derivations of its own input and could only ever report staleness, never divergence. It also contradicted two shipped contracts at once — the ledger is documented as the source of truth ("regenerate anyway, it is idempotent and cheap"), and the mirror is deliberately fail-OPEN at write, which yielded a second independent wedge where a swallowed mirror-write fault stranded the run at the next guarded command. A surface cannot be fail-open at write and fail-closed at read. Confirmed before deleting that no consumer reads the mirror's *content* for a decision (`sink-merge` matches a path string; `codex-compact-resume` builds a display line; the replan snapshot path re-verifies the archived copy against its own recorded digest), that no agent-facing surface is instructed to read it, and that `mutable_progress_digest` — whose `tasks:` term is dropped — has no consumers at all. Genuine corruption is still caught by the surviving tiers: a deleted, foreign-hash, or non-JSON mirror is now tolerated, while an illegal ledger progression, tampered plan bytes, and an unknown ledger status still refuse `state_ledger_progress_invalid`, `state_active_plan_invalid` and `state_ledger_authority_invalid` respectively. Separately, the four settlement envelopes that were missing `refreshTaskMirror` now carry it — applying the file's existing fail-open convention to the sites that were skipped, which closes the mirror-write-fault variant. Docs and the `sink-merge` fail-closed reason enumeration updated to match.

- **Re-plan `prepare` recovers the committed predecessor after a sanctioned scratch reset — #732.** A schema-2 replan starting from parent epoch ≥ 2 draws its predecessor only from the currently-live `.cache/replan-transaction.json`; a sanctioned scratch reset removes that file, so `prepareReplanUnlocked` fell through to `predecessor: null` (and lost `source.rotated_from` with it), never consulting `.cache/committed-transactions/` where the committed transaction is durably archived. It then returned `prepared` — a false success — because it never validates what it writes, and the wedge surfaced only on the next read, where the schema-2 lineage validator correctly refused `replan_transaction_predecessor_invalid`. Worse, the transaction-id preimage covers neither lost field, so the re-prepare produced a same-id, different-content transaction that silently overwrote the durable record. `prepare` now recovers the predecessor from the committed history, but only when EXACTLY ONE archived entry proves all five of: child plan hash equals the current parent plan hash, child epoch is exactly the current parent epoch, claim identity and lineage agree with active state, the committed transaction receipt is valid, and the archived source receipt matches its recorded digest byte-for-byte. Zero or multiple matches fail closed with `replan_committed_predecessor_unresolved` (distinguishing `no_committed_predecessor` from `ambiguous_committed_predecessor` and reporting the match count) — never a guess. The recovery reuses the existing `readCommittedTransactionAuthority` and `describeCommittedRotation` proofs rather than adding parallel digest machinery, and runs after the consent/budget block so a consent halt still has zero side effects. One boundary is deliberately left fail-closed: rotation into the committed-history directory happens lazily at the next prepare, so a reset that deletes a committed-but-never-rotated transaction is unrecoverable and correctly lands in the zero-match refusal. Canonical `replan.js`; all three edition ports confirmed to carry the change per-file. Fixtures are driven through the real prepare→resume transaction so the seals are genuine.

- **Adaptive review gates no longer settle a failed verdict with an empty canonical frontier — #728.** A schema-2 change gate could durably record a FAILED / REFUTED review attempt carrying zero canonical findings: `parseReviewEvidence` harvests only `finding_json:` rows, so a schema-1 flat `finding: id=… scope=…` row is invisible on the schema-2 close path; the non-adversarial coherence pair is skipped for `adversarial-verifier`; and `requiredReviewTokens` never asks an adversarial verifier for a finding token. So `domain_outcome: refuted` with no canonical finding parsed clean and settled `outcome: fail` with `findings: []`. Because convergence is measured by comparing successive frontiers by UID, `[]` vs `[]` read as `review_frontier_nonprogress`, and two of those as `review_nonconvergent` + `replan_required` — forcing a replan that carried no record of the defect it failed on. The settlement seam (`beginSchema2ReviewAttempt`) now refuses `review_settlement_missing_findings` when the **reduced aggregate** gate effect is `fail` and the canonical open frontier is empty; nothing is written to the review journal, and the refusal names the aggregate `domain_outcome` plus the `incoherent_members`. **The invariant binds the AGGREGATE, not the member — this is the correction over the first, reverted attempt, which bound it at the member seam and hard-refused a legal fan-out.** A member receipt that is individually `gate_effect: fail` — an abstaining `indeterminate` replica, or a minority refutation — but which a `replicated_majority` / `partitioned_all` reduction absorbs into a PASSING aggregate stays legal and closes normally, with no new obligation on that reviewer, so it cannot be coerced into fabricating a `failure_class` for a defect it could not determine. The guard keys on `gate_effect`, not `outcome`, so a progress-only closure failure (the reducer passes but a resolution proof is missing) still legitimately settles with an empty frontier. A passing gate with zero findings and a well-formed `finding_json:` receipt are both unchanged. A lone sequence adversarial-verifier that returns `indeterminate` with no finding is now also refused — the aggregate equals the single member, and a verdictless failure poisons convergence identically. Canonical `adaptive-node.js`; codex twin byte-copied; gitlab/gitea ports carry byte-identical added lines (confirmed per-file, not inferred from a green sync check).

- **Re-plan `resume` no longer wedges when the planner attests the authored (unfrozen) child — #737.** The planner's documented authoring target is the *unfrozen* `workflow-plan.next.md`, and the freeze belongs to `resume` — which stamps `plan_hash` and (on schema-2) seeds the compliance section, so the authored and frozen digests differ *by construction*. Both child-identity seams nonetheless required the attested digest to equal the FROZEN digest, so an authored attestation could never match; and because the refusal lands at phase `child_frozen`, every subsequent resume re-entered that phase and refused again — a permanent wedge with no repair path. The freeze already computes and verifies both digests, so the transaction now records that witness pair once (`child_authored` carrying the authored digest, the authored bytes, and the frozen digest) and each seam is a pure digest comparison requiring exact equality on BOTH. The record is refreshed whenever the attested digest changes, so the documented bounded child-repair loop — author, refuse, re-author, re-attest, resume — converges inside one transaction instead of pinning the first draft forever. **Every seam stays a pure function of bytes already recorded: no identity check calls `freezePlan`, `validatePlan`, the filesystem, or the live worktree.** That constraint is now a pinned regression, not a convention — an earlier iteration of this fix re-derived the stamp by calling `freezePlan` at the archive seam, which re-runs full freeze-mode validation against the live tree and made a SEALED, immutable epoch snapshot flip to unverifiable when an unrelated path in the working tree changed, with no way to repair the archive. A test now seals an epoch, mutates the live tree, and asserts the snapshot still verifies. The legacy path where a planner attests the already-stamped child is byte-for-byte unchanged (it short-circuits on the first comparison), and a pre-existing in-flight transaction back-fills the record on its next resume. Adversarial verification ran an independent seven-mutation matrix — including both regressions earlier iterations were rejected for — and every illegitimate input it constructed (post-attestation tampering, a divergent on-disk child, forged recorded bytes, a forged sealed witness preserving the plan_hash) still fails closed.

- **Interior-gate freshness no longer trusts the seal-time surface map verbatim, and a group certifier no longer counts as a live whole-candidate wall — #750, #751.** *(#750)* Scoped interior-gate freshness compares a seal-time blob map against the current tree, but that map (`attempt.candidate_declared`) was read verbatim from the review journal, so editing one entry made a genuinely changed file look unchanged and re-freshened a stale gate. The map is now corroborated against the gate's per-member barrier baseline before the scoped path is used, and any gate whose map cannot be corroborated falls back to whole-candidate binding. **This is a privilege raise, not a tamper-proof seal, and is documented as such:** defeating it now requires git write access to rewrite the anchored baseline ref, not merely a journal edit — verified by escalating attack, where a journal-only tamper and a journal-plus-forged-base-file tamper both still refuse, while an attacker who can also rewrite the ref succeeds. That is the same trust model `--barrier-check` itself already runs on. *(#751)* `finalCertifierLive()` mirrored the finalize sweep's status filter but not its branch: with an epoch contract active, a reviewer *fanout* certifier routes to the group arm, which performs no candidate-staleness comparison at all — yet it was still counted as a live whole-candidate wall, which is what licenses interior gates to drop to scoped freshness. A group certifier no longer qualifies, so those interior gates keep their whole-candidate binding. This is the tighten-only option and needs no change to the group arm itself. **Neither change re-introduces the false blocks this machinery exists to prevent:** verification built its own over-refusal fixtures beyond the shipped tests — a three-member `replicated_majority` adversarial-verifier fanout driven through the real runtime, followed by a disjoint downstream write, still passes with all per-member baselines corroborated — and the shipped controls pin that an untampered journal keeps its scoped relief across sequence and fanout shapes, both reviewer roles, schema-1 and epoch-child.

- **`--finalize-check`'s attribution sweep now unions the epoch lineage too, completing the multi-epoch finalize fix — #753.** The barrier fix (#724) unioned the sealed parent-epoch write sets at `--barrier-check`, but the finalize attribution sweep still built its `completeDeclared` set from `parseNodes(content)` — the child plan alone — and refused `unattributed_change` on the same parent-epoch paths. So a replanned run cleared the barrier and was then blocked at finalize on exactly the paths the barrier had just accepted: two gates, one candidate diff, two different attribution sets. The #724 lineage resolution is now lifted into one module-level `resolveEpochLineagePlans(content, planPath)` consumed by BOTH arms — a genuine dedup rather than a second, subtly-different walker — with the barrier arm's refusal wording byte-unchanged. The same guarantees carry over: gated on `epoch_contract active && plan_epoch >= 2 && parent_plan_hash` present (schema-1 and epoch-1 plans take the identical old path); reuses `verifyAllEpochSnapshots`; selects epochs by integer index, never by `parent_plan_hash`; refuses `epoch_lineage_unverified` before the sweep when the lineage cannot be verified, rather than mislabelling tampering as `unattributed_change`; and — the load-bearing half — folds parent nodes into the attribution map keyed to their own epoch's ledger status, so a parent-declared path whose parent row was never `complete` still surfaces as unattributed. Adversarial verification ran a four-level tamper (widen a `complete` parent's write set, repair the manifest entry, recompute the manifest self-digest, repoint the active-manifest digest) and it still refuses `snapshot_authority_projection_mismatch` because the projection is recomputed from the archived plan bytes; five independent mutants (including "union declarations but ignore the per-epoch ledger" and "ignore the snapshot-verify failure") were all caught; and legitimate finalize (child-declared paths, no-op diffs) still passes. With this, the multi-epoch finalize symptom is fully resolved end-to-end.

- **Whole-plan `--barrier-check` now unions the sealed parent-epoch write sets across the epoch lineage — #724.** A schema-2 child plan produced by repair→replan carries an ACCUMULATED candidate (the branch diff includes parent-epoch writes), but the whole-plan arm built its declared allowlist from the child plan's own `## Nodes` alone, so files legitimately declared and gated by a parent-epoch node refused `write_set_overflow` at the finalize-entry gate. Reproduced against the real archived multi-epoch artifact: child 9 declared paths, epoch-2 9, epoch-1 13, union 14 — matching the reporter's "14/14 actual writes covered", with exactly five production paths declared only by a parent-epoch node whose own ledger row is `complete`. **Widening a safety allowlist made snapshot integrity load-bearing, so the union is gated on proof, not on presence.** `--barrier-check` is a separate CLI invocation from `--finalize-check` and previously read no `## Meta`, no `.cache/epochs/`, and no manifest at all — and `.cache/**` sits inside the barrier's own exempt allowband, so a hand-edited parent snapshot would have been invisible to the very gate it widened. The CLI now runs the existing `verifyAllEpochSnapshots` lineage walk (reused, not reimplemented — it already recomputes each epoch's authority projection from the archived plan BYTES and binds contiguity, lineage id, claim root, active manifest digest and live plan hash) and refuses `epoch_lineage_unverified` before `barrierCheck` runs, carrying replan's own typed reason in a sibling field so `reason` stays one stable token. That placement is deliberate: falling through with a child-only allowlist would refuse `write_set_overflow`, which is a lie — it reports an authoring error when the real fault is snapshot tampering, and the documented operator response to `write_set_overflow` is `revert-overflow`, which would destroy legitimate parent-epoch work. Lineage members are selected by INTEGER INDEX from the verified snapshot array, never by matching `parent_plan_hash`, because `computePlanHash` covers only `## Meta` + `## Nodes` (+ `## Node Briefs`) and is therefore not a unique byte pin. **The union does not create a laundering primitive**, and the second half of the change is what keeps it honest: parent nodes are also folded into the rank-4 `anyCompleteOwner` attribution map keyed to their own epoch's ledger status, so a parent-declared path whose parent row was never `complete` lands in the existing `unattributed_write` arm rather than bypassing the floor — same teeth, no new reason code, no new precedence rank. `barrierCheck` stays IO-free; all lineage IO happens in the CLI arm; per-node and `--group-barrier` modes are untouched, since a node writing a parent-epoch file is still that node's own overflow. With `lineagePlans` absent, behavior is byte-identical for every existing caller. Adversarial verification attacked the widening directly and it held: a tampered parent snapshot widening the parent write set to an attacker path refuses `epoch_lineage_unverified` and never accepts the path; a bogus extra epoch directory refuses `snapshot_epoch_sequence_invalid`; a path declared by no epoch still refuses `write_set_overflow`; and a parent-declared path whose parent ledger reads `n/a` surfaces as `unattributed_write`. Test fixtures are driven through the REAL replan transaction so the epoch seals are genuine rather than hand-written to look sealed. Canonical, Codex, GitLab and Gitea plan-validator editions synchronized.

- **A mixed direct-repair-then-replay history at ONE review gate no longer wedges the review journal permanently — #748.** The in-plan descendant replay shipped in 6.24.0 (#739) performs a journal-GLOBAL, plan-GLOBAL mutation: it reopens the replay owner to `in_progress` and resets every completed non-gate writer descendant in its cone to `pending`. The exemption that lets the identity validators TOLERATE that reset, however, was ATTEMPT-LOCAL — read from each attempt's own `replay` record. Because `reviewJournalIdentityMatchesPlan` deliberately RECOMPUTES every attempt's producer proof against the live ledger on every read (the "never trust the record" anti-laundering design), a SETTLED direct-repair attempt already recorded at the same gate recomputed with both of its bound producers now non-terminal — the replay owner `in_progress` and not that attempt's writer, the reset descendant `pending` — and refused `review_journal_repair_identity_mismatch`. `readReviewJournal` is the single choke point, so *every* sanctioned continuation died on it: `repair-node` resume, `reopen-node`, `record-evidence` for the very node the replay reopened, the fused advance, and — because `runRepairNodeCore` refuses before it can ever return `repair_requires_replan` — the replan escape hatch too, which then re-refuses `replan_source_authority_invalid` on its own second journal read. Pre-#739 this exact input escalated to replan with zero mutation; post-#739 it mutated the plan and then dead-ended, with no in-place recovery. The trigger is the ordinary case, not an exotic one: it needs only ≥1 settled direct repair plus ≥1 active replay at one gate (multiple findings with different owners), exactly the shape the live phase-run evidence shows. Repeated replays by the SAME owner never wedged. Fix: the relief is now journal-GLOBAL and proof-BOUNDED. A new `activeReplayRelief` pre-pass walks every attempt in the journal, keeps only records that pass the full `validatedReplayExempt` recompute (owner is a real node with a nonempty write set; every listed descendant lies in the owner's `structuralNonGateWriterDescendants` cone on the FROZEN graph; the owner uniquely owns that attempt's own frozen blocking findings; the attempt's bound writer IS the owner), bounds each surviving record's LIFETIME, and feeds the survivors to every attempt's proof — the owner via `preservedProducers` (which relaxes only `in_progress` and does NOT bypass the graph-maximal check) and the reset descendants via a dedicated `statusRelief` parameter (they sit at `pending`, which `preserved` cannot relieve). **`statusRelief` is deliberately separate from the attempt-local `replayExempt`, and that separation is load-bearing.** The journal-global set carries exactly one power — STATUS admission — because that is the only thing the replay's ledger mutation actually justifies journal-wide. `replayExempt` additionally waives the graph-MAXIMAL binding check, which is what pins each attempt to the unique maximal writer it really repaired; merging the journal-global set into it (the first version of this fix) let one live replay retire the writer-binding proof of every OTHER attempt in the journal, so a tampered settled attempt claiming it had repaired an unrelated upstream writer read back clean. Split apart, that tamper refuses `review_journal_repair_identity_mismatch` while the untampered mixed history still reads `ok`, and the ancestry-bypass case is pinned. The schema-2 twin `reviewJournalV2MatchesPlan` gets the identical relief for derived (`certified_producers`-empty) gates. **The lifetime bound is SUPERSESSION, not "healed".** A record is retired for good once a strictly higher `ordinal` exists at the same `logical_gate.key`: the gate can only mint a later attempt after its cone re-completed and it re-dispatched, so a superseded replay is provably finished history, while a genuinely live mid-replay record is never superseded. The all-`complete` cone check is only a per-read suspension — on its own, any later un-healing would re-arm the relief, so one legitimate replay would have made every node in its descendant cone permanently `pending`-launderable at that gate, for every attempt, forever. That fail-open was found by adversarial verification of this fix and is pinned closed in both the schema-1 and schema-2 twins (a third attempt after the replay, then a hand-edited cone member, must still refuse `review_journal_repair_identity_mismatch`), with a control proving an UNsuperseded replay still relieves its own reset cone. **Nothing is loosened beyond that whitelist:** an unsound replay record contributes NOTHING and is still refused by the per-attempt loop, so every existing typed refusal keeps its precedence; a removed/emptied/forged-owner/out-of-cone/malformed replay record, a deleted producer binding, a re-snapshotted baseline, the rebind lineage chain, gate identity, attempt-id and ordinal contiguity all still refuse; on an untampered journal relief can only move a `producer_slice` toward the statically-complete producer set `producer_bindings` was frozen from, never past it (a tampered journal is caught by the recompute itself, not by that ordering); and a journal with no `replay` record anywhere is byte-identical to prior behavior. Canonical `scripts/kaola-workflow-adaptive-node.js`; codex twin byte-copied; gitlab/gitea ports regenerated. Coverage pays down the replay debt the issue named: the mixed-history regression pin plus run-to-green, the corruption battery (unproven cone reset, forged record ×4, deleted binding, owner stranded at `pending`), the same-owner control, the schema-2 derived-gate twin, the first walkthrough replay scenario (`testMixedRepairReplayJournal748`, end-to-end through the real plan-run surface — the walkthrough previously had zero replay coverage), the three previously-untested replay decline reasons (`finding_outside_cone`, `owner_not_gate_producer`, `replay_cone_synthesis_boundary`) pinned through `runRepairNode` with zero-mutation assertions, and the replay-vs-`REVIEW_REPAIR_LIMIT` budget pin proving a replay gets no free lane.

- **Interior `code-reviewer` / `security-reviewer` walls no longer false-block finalization when a disjoint downstream write moves the candidate — #745 (partial).** The interior-gate surface-freshness rescue shipped in 6.24.0 accepted only `adversarial-verifier` gates, so an interior reviewer wall kept its whole-candidate binding: once it sealed PASS, any later write anywhere in the tree — including one touching none of the files it reviewed — moved the whole candidate, staled its receipt, and refused `--verdict-check` with `verdict_not_pass` / "schema-2 certifier receipt is stale for the current candidate", wedging finalization. Reviewer roles are forbidden from declaring `certifies` at freeze time (G4) and therefore carry no explicit producer list, which is why the original rescue could not cover them. The gate's reviewed-producer slice is now RECOMPUTED from the frozen graph plus the ledger — the common-ancestor writer slice of the gate's members, minus `n/a` rows, mirroring the runtime's own `uniqueMaximalReviewProducer` derivation — and the seal attempt's recorded `producer_bindings` must match it exactly. Freshness is then scoped to those producers' declared write sets using exactly the same seal-time-blob-vs-current-tree comparison the adversarial-verifier path already used. **What is and is not cross-checked, precisely:** the journal's `producer_bindings` — the field that decides WHICH files the scoped surface covers — is recomputed from the frozen plan and rejected on any disagreement, so a shrunk or widened binding set cannot move the surface. The seal-time blob map itself (`attempt.candidate_declared`), which decides whether those files have CHANGED, is still read verbatim from the same journal and is not independently cross-checked. That is pre-existing behavior inherited from the adversarial-verifier arm, not introduced here, and it is unchanged by this entry. The designated final `code_certifier` / `security_certifier` keeps its whole-candidate binding, and every shape that cannot be proven safe still fails closed to whole-candidate: a plan with no final certifier that is both resolvable AND live (nothing left holding the whole-candidate wall, so scoping any gate to its own surface would silently retire it), a missing review journal, no matching seal attempt, mixed candidate digests across a fan-out group, a gate membership or producer slice that disagrees with the recompute, a producer that is not `complete`, an empty producer slice or empty surface, and any git failure. A genuine change to a reviewed file still refuses stale — the widening is a real content check, not an always-pass. Both admitted reviewer roles are exercised end-to-end, and three fail-open regressions found by adversarial verification are pinned closed: a shrunk/tampered `producer_bindings` must not narrow the freshness surface; a plan whose final-certifier set resolves empty must not let an unreviewed file pass; and — the third, found by verifying the fix for the second — the final-certifier test is now LIVENESS, not resolvability. `finalCertifierIds()` is purely plan-structural: it never consults the ledger, so a certifier whose Node Ledger row reads `n/a` still "resolved" while the verdict sweep never checked it, nothing held the whole candidate, every interior reviewer fell through to surface scope, and an unreviewed new file finalized clean (`{"ok":true,"checked":["probe"]}`). The reviewer arm now requires at least one final certifier whose ledger row reads exactly `complete`. That is an ALLOW-list, deliberately mirroring the sweep's own membership test (`if (ledger.get(node.id) !== 'complete') continue;`) rather than a deny-list of "bad" statuses. A first attempt at this fix excluded only `n/a`, reasoning that `pending`/`in_progress` merely mean "the wall is still ahead" — that reasoning was wrong, and adversarial verification of the fix showed why: whole-plan `--verdict-check` is blocking at FINALIZE, and at the finalize sweep there is no ahead, so a certifier row at `pending` or `in_progress` is never checked either and holds no wall. With the deny-list version, an unreviewed rogue file passed all three blocking finalize checks behind a `pending` certifier (`{"ok":true,"checked":["probe"]}`); with the allow-list it refuses `verdict_not_pass`. Both residual statuses are now pinned for both reviewer roles, and the change costs nothing in over-refusal, because per-node `--verdict-check` is explicitly informational. This is the exact shape current interim guidance recommends ("prefer code-reviewer walls over adversarial-verifier change gates in schema-2 plans"), so the residual was likely to be hit in practice. Canonical, Codex, GitLab, and Gitea plan-validator editions synchronized.

- **`reopen-node` / `repair-node` now refuse `would_strand_completed_dependent` instead of silently creating an unrecoverable project.** `runReopenNode` folds only gate roles, and its orphan guard inspects only `in_progress` rows — so a `complete` non-gate descendant was neither folded nor seen. The reopen was ADMITTED, and it left that row sitting above a dependency chain the same call had just reset to `pending`: exactly the shape `verifyCurrentEpochAuthority` rejects as `state_ledger_progress_invalid`. Because the scripted commands consult that authority before mutating, the result was a project in which they refuse with `legal_mutation: "none"` and no sanctioned exit exists — reached with no warning whatsoever at mutation time. The `baseline_failed` crash residue lands in the same state. (Reproduced directly against `verifyCurrentEpochAuthority`: the wedged ledger yields `state_ledger_progress_invalid`; correcting the one offending row alone still yields `state_task_mirror_mismatch`; only after re-deriving the task mirror does the authority return `ok` — which is exactly the two-step recovery now documented.) **The guard is ROLE-AGNOSTIC, because the invariant it mirrors is.** `verifyCurrentEpochAuthority` refuses for ANY node at `complete`/`in_progress`/`n/a` whose dependency is neither `complete` nor `n/a`, whatever its role; a `role === 'finalize'` special case therefore both under-covered — the identical wedge stayed admissible for every interior node, reachable from an ordinary MID-RUN state with the sink still `pending` — and mis-described, since a plan with a second finalize-role node produced a refusal calling an interior node "the finalize sink". Both twins now simulate the post-mutation ledger (the reopened node to `in_progress`, the folded gates — and, for a `#739` replay, its reset descendant cone — to `pending`) and refuse if any node that would remain settled is left above an unsatisfied dependency, naming the offenders in `detail`. **The refusal is scoped to what the mutation NEWLY strands, and it folds rather than refuses whatever it can fold.** "Newly" is decided by ATTRIBUTION: a row refuses only when it would remain settled above a dependency THIS mutation moved (the reopened node, the reset rows, the read-only folds), so a violation that was ALREADY in the plan — a settled row above a pending dependency in a DISJOINT leg this reopen never touches — no longer blocks an otherwise-safe, entirely unrelated reopen, and the scan is confined to the touched cone by construction. Attribution rather than a raw before/after set difference, deliberately: subtracting the pre-mutation stranded set would have retired the repair-node arm of the guard wholesale, because `repair-node`'s safe point runs with a downstream gate at `in_progress` — which is not in the SATISFIED set — so every settled descendant above that gate already reads as "stranded before", and the complete-sink-behind-a-live-gate wedge the guard exists to refuse would have been admitted as pre-existing. Both properties are pinned (the disjoint-leg admission and the complete-sink refusal). And a `complete` descendant whose declared write set is EMPTY produced no repo bytes — nothing to undo, which is precisely why folding a post-dominating GATE is safe — so it is FOLDED to `pending` alongside the gates (to a fixpoint, because folding one row can strand the next), with its stale `.cache/<id>.md` evidence and `barrier-base-<id>` removed exactly like a folded gate's. Refusing on it instead made the upstream writer PERMANENTLY unrepairable in-plan: for `a → impl → review → notes(read-only) → finalize` with `notes` complete, neither `reopen-node impl` nor `repair-node impl` was admissible from any clean ledger state — a capability regression, not a tightening. The `finalize` role is EXCLUDED from that fold even when its declared write set is empty: the sink runs main-session-direct and its first irreversible step (the feature commit) writes neither node evidence nor a sink-receipt journal, so nothing in-band distinguishes a pristine sink from one that already committed/pushed/merged, and folding it could double-apply irreversible work — a settled sink still refuses, with the replan exit as its sanctioned recovery. The status sets also accept the same defensive `n/a` spellings (`n.a`, `na`) as `TERMINAL_LEDGER` elsewhere in the file, so a row spelled `na` reads as settled/satisfied instead of yielding a spurious refusal. **Pre-write, precisely:** in `reopen-node` the refusal fires before any side effect at all — zero ledger mutation, zero baseline unlink, zero shell. In `repair-node` it fires before every ledger, baseline, evidence and journal-settlement mutation of the repair itself, but the shared journal preamble that runs ahead of it (the STEP-15 original-barrier replay shell, and `reconcilePendingRebind`'s crash convergence on the interrupted-rebind path) may already have written; moving the guard above that preamble would have reordered a long, heavily pinned typed-refusal precedence ladder, which is not a trade worth making for a guard that is already ahead of every mutation it exists to prevent. Its `operator_hint` states the EMPIRICALLY VERIFIED preconditions of the sanctioned exit rather than asserting one that may itself refuse: `prepareReplan` was run against an all-complete post-finalize fixture and refuses `replan_source_journal_missing` with no `.cache/review-attempts.json`, `replan_source_outcome_missing` with the journal but no valid `.cache/replan-source.json` `repair_outcome` handoff, and prepares only when BOTH are present (an all-complete ledger is not itself a blocker) — so the hint names those two preconditions. **The hint is state-aware, because it fires from two structurally different states.** Its terminal clause used to claim unconditionally that "the run is finished: treat the work as shipped and open a follow-up issue instead" — false from the MID-RUN trigger, where the sink is still `pending`, nothing has shipped, and that advice forecloses the in-plan continuation. It now branches on whether the terminal sink itself reached `complete`: complete → the run really is finished and the follow-up advice stands; anything else → it says so plainly and points at repairing the TAIL-most settled writer instead (no resolvable sink reads as "not finished", never claiming work shipped that may not have). **`complete`, deliberately not the strand-SATISFIED set.** That set also contains `n/a` (and its `n.a` / `na` spellings), because an inapplicable row legitimately sits above a reset dependency — but an `n/a` sink means the sink was marked NOT APPLICABLE, so nothing shipped at all, and telling the operator to "treat the work as shipped and open a follow-up issue" would have been false AND would have foreclosed the in-plan recovery. Every sink status is now pinned against the emitted hint text, for both the reopen and repair arms. Its "do NOT hand-edit a ledger row" clause is likewise scoped to the state it fires in — no ledger row moved, so the on-disk ledger is still consistent and there is nothing wedged to repair by hand. **That pre-write clause branches on the OP, because the two twins are not equally pre-write and a hint must not promise what only one of them delivers.** Only the refusal's VERB used to branch; the clause itself asserted unconditionally that the refusal "fired BEFORE any mutation", which is exactly true for `reopen-node` and false for `repair-node` — where, as the paragraph above already records, `reconcilePendingRebind` (review-journal write, baseline `writeFile`, `git update-ref`) and the STEP-15 barrier shell both run ahead of the guard. The repair arm now states the accurate, narrower claim: the repair's own mutations were not applied, and a shared preamble of the same call may already have written, which is expected and self-converges on retry. The reopen arm keeps the strong claim verbatim. That is not in conflict with the manual two-step recipe in `docs/workflow-state-contract.md`, which addresses the disjoint state of a project ALREADY wedged by a runtime predating this guard; both surfaces now state which state they apply to, and `docs/architecture.md` — which still described the REJECTED before/after set-difference algorithm and claimed both guards fire before any side effect — is corrected to the shipped attribution design with the repair-arm qualification. Strictly tighten-only: no exemption, no park, no reset of the stranded row, and `verifyCurrentEpochAuthority` is untouched; the change only refuses earlier and more clearly. Symmetry is pinned so it cannot over-refuse: a `pending` sink stays ADMISSIBLE (the designed mid-run repair, still performing its full narrow reset), an `in_progress` sink keeps its pre-existing `would_orphan_in_progress` with unchanged precedence, and reopening the completed sink ITSELF stays admissible (a node is not its own descendant). An `n/a` sink DOES now refuse, which is a deliberate widening over the role-specific guard: checked directly against `verifyCurrentEpochAuthority`, `sink=n/a` + `gate=pending` refuses `state_ledger_progress_invalid` exactly as `sink=complete` + `gate=pending` does, so admitting it would leave the identical unrecoverable wedge. A pre-existing test pin asserting that transitive readiness "withholds" a *completed* sink was false — a complete row is not withheld, it is stranded — and its fixture now exercises the narrow-reset property for real, via a COMPLETE parallel branch (its own writer and its own non-post-dominating gate) that must survive the reopen untouched — and its sink row's `depends_on` cell, first written space-separated, now uses the comma `parseNodes` actually splits on (space-separated parsed to the single nonexistent dep `reviewsib-review`, silently un-sinking the plan; the pin still fails under a widened reset, so it stays non-tautological). `docs/workflow-state-contract.md` documents the two-step manual recovery for a project already wedged by a runtime installed at 6.24.0.

- **Replay relief is sourced ONLY from the population the read itself re-proves — #748 (structural narrowing).** `activeReplayRelief` read the journal through `reviewJournalAttempts()`, which unions `journal.attempts` with the imported `__legacy_attempts` projection. Only the former is subjected to the per-attempt identity recompute; imported records are validated for ordinal contiguity alone. A forged or imported legacy replay record could therefore grant relief that no read had ever proven — a fail-open, and the FIFTH consecutive defect of the identical shape (relief trusting an over-broad population: all attempts, then superseded attempts, then the ancestry proof, then legacy imports). Rather than add a fifth narrowing special case, the population itself is now the bound, stated as one invariant in the code: **relief can only ever come from a record this read has independently re-proven.** Concretely, only `journal.attempts` may CONTRIBUTE relief; legacy imports are excluded outright, and if imported history ever needs to carry relief it must first be subjected to the same per-attempt recompute, not admitted by widening the population. Legacy records still count toward the supersession MAXIMUM, because that direction is tighten-only — a higher imported ordinal may retire a current record, never revive one. Paid down with the coverage whose absence let this shape recur: the single function behind every relief defect had no DIRECT unit coverage, only end-to-end exercise. It now has a unit block pinning each bound independently — a live unsuperseded replay relieves exactly its own cone; a superseded one grants nothing; a legacy/unvalidated record grants nothing; nine forged/unprovable shapes grant nothing; a healed cone suspends; and the returned relief, fed to the real `uniqueMaximalReviewProducer`, admits the moved rows without waiving the graph-maximal proof while the same set routed through `replayExempt` does waive it. Every case is RED against a mutant that removes its own bound.

- **"Empty declared write set" is now ONE predicate instead of two that disagreed.** `isReadOnlyNode` and the writer predicate `nodeWriteSetNonempty` encoded the same concept ~5,000 lines apart with two different token lists: the writer predicate treated `none` and `n/a` as no-write markers, while `isReadOnlyNode` delegated to `parseWriteSetCell`, which parses them as a one-path write set. A node whose declared write set cell literally reads `none` or `n/a` was therefore classified read-only by every producer/graph computation but NOT by the read-only fold — so a settled such descendant over-refused `would_strand_completed_dependent` instead of folding, the exact class the read-only fold exists to close. A differential harness could not see it, because both sides of the diff shared the predicate. `isReadOnlyNode` is now the exact negation of `nodeWriteSetNonempty`, which itself folds the authored no-write markers together with `parseWriteSetCell`'s structural parse — a reduction to one definition, with no third token list anywhere. Pinned: a complete descendant whose cell is `none` (and `n/a`) FOLDS.

- **`repair-node` and `reopen-node` now fold identically.** Two divergences between the twins are closed. (1) `reopen-node`'s adversarial-verifier fan-out receipt purge iterates the FULL fold set (`rowsReset` = structural gates PLUS the read-only rows folded with them); `repair-node`'s iterated `gatesReset` only. A verifier declares no writes, so a non-post-dominating fan-out gate enters the fold through the read-only path — and `repair-node` was leaving its stale skeptic receipts on disk, where a later `--verdict-check` could still consume a pass that never certified the repaired tree. Verified by probe, not assumed: the same fixture yields `evidenceRemoved: []` before and both fan-out receipts after. (2) `repair-node` derived its `uniqueSink` as the FIRST node with no descendants under a comment claiming it matched `reopen-node`, which in fact returns null unless there is EXACTLY ONE terminal. With `runIsFinished` now consuming that value, the intended "no resolvable sink ⇒ conservatively not finished" path was unreachable in the repair arm, and the post-dominance test could assert dominance of a path other terminals bypass. `repair-node` now derives it exactly as `reopen-node` does and takes the same conservative fallback at the post-dominance site (`no unique sink ⇒ treat the gate as gating`); the comment is corrected.

- **Schema-2 repair→replan handoffs no longer refuse at the source-authority check (#720, #734).** `readSource` verified a schema-2 parent review attempt against two legacy shapes, which made the repair→replan transaction unreachable for every current plan. The candidate check compared the attempt's sealed digest against the legacy raw ls-tree digest while the schema-2 close path had re-based onto the validation runner's landable-tree digest — two algorithms differing on both serialization and filter band, which can never agree, so `prepare` always refused `replan_source_candidate_changed`. The per-receipt evidence check read only the schema-1 `body`/`receipt_sha256` pair, so a schema-2 receipt binding evidence through `raw_evidence_sha256` always refused `replan_source_evidence_mismatch`. Contract-2 attempts are now compared against `computeLandableTreeDigest` scoped by the parent plan's `validation_test_consumes`, and both receipt bindings are checked conjunctively — so a receipt carrying a forged self-consistent legacy pair beside a valid raw digest is still refused, where a shape-selecting branch would have admitted it. Contract-1 attempts keep the raw comparison and the legacy binding, byte-unchanged, discriminated per-ATTEMPT rather than by the journal's `schema_version`.

- **`release` no longer reports a discard archive committed while the move is half-done (#749).** `commitDiscardArchive` staged only the archive DESTINATION, so for a tracked live folder the additions were committed while the source deletions stayed unstaged — `discard_archive_committed:true` alongside five tracked deletions still in the working tree, requiring a manual follow-up commit. The source path is now staged with the destination when it is tracked (conditionally: `git add -A -- <no-match>` exits 128, which would otherwise flip the common untracked-folder case to a false `committed:false`), and the existing post-commit truth check is extended so `committed:true` additionally requires the tracked source to be absent at `HEAD`.

- **A legacy claim can no longer freeze a current adaptive plan it could never replan (#749).** Authoring admission is stateless, and the fresh-freeze path never verified epoch authority, so a pre-epoch-envelope claim could reach `ready_to_run` and freeze a schema-2 plan — after which the claim-preserving replan transaction could not construct a child epoch from that legacy authority, and recovery required release/archive/reclaim plus a second planner run. The handoff now refuses `legacy_claim_upgrade_required` before any plan mutation when the state carries no epoch envelope, directing the caller to release and re-claim (claiming already writes a complete, correct envelope). Zero-mutation refusal, chosen over an atomic-promotion mechanism that cannot soundly reconstruct a historical claim root spanning more than one commit.

- **`sink-merge` no longer reports `status:"sinked"` while silently skipping archive and roadmap (#746).** The finalize step treated an epoch-authority refusal carrying an empty `missing[]` as the historical benign journal-only skip, so a swallowed refusal was reported as a completed sink. The discriminator now distinguishes an evidence-losing archive from a swallowed authority refusal and fails loudly, so `sinked` implies the archive exists and the roadmap source was removed. The benign journal-only skip is preserved and pinned, so the guard does not over-tighten.

- **`gap-sweep`'s `## Run gaps` sample delimitation (#726).** The `<sample>` group used a negated class (`[^)]+`), so any sample containing `)` never parsed and `--check` refused `gaps_unswept` for a gap the operator had already mapped. The group is now **lazy** (`(.+?)`), not greedy: a greedy `(.+)` fixes that case but backtracks to the *last* `"): "` in the line, mis-carving a legal free-text `noise:` justification containing `"): filed: #N"` into the sample and refusing `observed_gap_unseeded` against text the operator never wrote — the same failure mode relocated. Lazy takes the leftmost `"): "` followed by a valid `filed:`/`noise:` tail and handles both shapes; a 700,000-line differential fuzz confirms it is a strict, capture-preserving superset of the previous grammar.

- **A fresh epoch-1 schema-2 plan no longer freezes without its `## Required Agent Compliance` section (#719).** Such a plan carries `plan_schema_version: 2` but *not* `epoch_schema_version` — that field appears only on replan child plans — and both compliance walls (`validatePlan` at freeze, `revalidateForResume` at resume) are gated on `parseEpochContract(content).active`, so both skipped the check and the plan froze with the section absent entirely. The runtime epoch-authority check is **not** gated that way: for an epoch-planned state it validates the section unconditionally, so it refused perfectly legitimate mid-run operations — `replan prepare`, finalize archive, and `discard`/`release` — with `state_compliance_authority_invalid`. `freezePlan` now producer-completes the artifact at its authoring boundary: one `pending` row per node in `## Nodes` order, including the finalize/sink node, emitted through the same `schema.spliceComplianceSection` producer that `addCloseCompliance` later advances in place, so a mid-run close or replay stays idempotent and never appends a duplicate. Seeding is inject-if-absent and schema-2 only, applied *after* the hash is stamped: a plan that already carries the section (a replan child, or a re-freeze with advanced rows) is left byte-identical, a legacy v1 plan is never seeded, and the section sits outside `computePlanHash`, so the change is plan_hash-neutral and `--governance-ack`'s hash assertion and `--resume-check` are unaffected. Teaching the shared authority check to tolerate an absent section was rejected as the alternative: tolerance would blind it to genuine mid-run corruption, which is a looser gate rather than an equivalent one.

- **A user-consented abandon no longer demands run-state artifacts the project never produced — #735.** `discard` / `release` routed through the composed archive authority check, which requires a full one-row-per-node compliance section and a ledger-matching task mirror. A schema-2 adaptive project has neither while frozen-but-never-run, and only partial compliance rows mid-run, so a consented abandon had no working CLI path at all — the refusal also skipped worktree, branch and claim-label cleanup, leaving the operator to finish the run or clean up by hand. Abandon-time archival now downgrades the authority reasons that describe *missing run product* rather than tampering. This is safe on the axis that matters, and that was checked rather than assumed: a healthy abandon **already** deletes an unmerged feature branch (`removeBranch` is `git branch -D`), so the authority gate was never a work-preservation guard, and the archive relocates all state intact rather than deleting it. The archived destination is re-verified with the **unrelaxed** helper, so a downgraded abandon records its lineage as `failed` and the preservation token cannot be laundered.

### Changed

- **The `adversarial-verifier` role now has a discovery/closure behavior contract — #731.** The reviewer-v2 engine distinguishes `review_phase: discovery` from `closure`, and the `code-reviewer` profile has stated that policy since the discovery/closure work shipped — but the `adversarial-verifier` profile never did. Since one counterexample is logically sufficient to refute a claim, the profile permitted discovery to stop at the first blocker, the writer to repair it, and closure to then run another unrestricted whole-surface search that surfaced a *different* latent blocker — serializing real defects one repair cycle at a time. Observed live: discovery found three findings, then successive closures produced five more independent, reproducible defects one at a time, each closure re-falsifying the entire claim rather than the prior frontier plus the repair delta. The role was doing exactly what its prompt allowed. Discovery is now required to inspect the declared `gate_surface` once and establish the full admitted frontier before returning — continuing past the first successful counterexample through the material predeclared falsification categories, consolidating manifestations of one root cause, and recording each independent demonstrated counterexample as its own canonical finding — explicitly bounded by the supplied surface and the existing wait budget, not an invitation to unbounded or speculative findings. Closure must account for every prior finding UID, reproduce the prior frontier, inspect the repair delta, and route a genuine new blocker outside that delta to `review_scope_expanded` (planner-owned replan) instead of silently widening the same direct-repair lineage. Authored in the shared `behavior-contracts.json` source and regenerated into the Claude Markdown and Codex TOML surfaces, so the two runtimes cannot drift; the generator's own check confirms the surfaces are generated rather than hand-edited. This is the role-behavior half of the loop whose harness half shipped as the empty-frontier settlement guard.

- **The read-only lane classification for a node declaring `none` / `n/a` is now pinned (#752).** Unifying the empty-write-set predicate (`isReadOnlyNode` became the exact negation of `nodeWriteSetNonempty`) reached beyond the fold it was written for and also changed how the running-set scheduler classifies such a node: `parseWriteSetCell` has no case for those words and returns a one-element set containing the fictional path `none`, so the node was previously scheduled as a WRITER. The folded classification is kept — it matches authorial intent, makes `none` behave exactly like the already-shared `—`, and matches the plan-validator slice whose comment explicitly promises non-divergence from the runtime predicate — and the previous behavior was in fact worse: it formed a lane group whose barrier allowlist contained two paths that do not exist and provisioned real git worktree legs for nodes that write nothing. **No protection is lost**, because write-set truthfulness is enforced elsewhere and unconditionally: the close barrier builds its allowlist straight from `parseWriteSetCell` with no read/write branch, so a `none`-declaring node that writes a real file still refuses `write_set_overflow`. This entry is test-only (no production change): a scheduler-level scenario asserts a `none` node and an `n/a` node open on the read lane at the read-only cap with no lane group, while a node with a real single-path write set still opens on the write lane. The mutation that reverts `isReadOnlyNode` to its pre-unification body flips the first two and provably leaves the third unchanged, so the pin discriminates rather than passing for an incidental reason. Note the residual, which this does not change: `none` / `n/a` remain freeze-legal spellings that parse to a fictional one-path write set, so such a node's declared allowlist is `{"none"}` rather than empty — harmless, since a fictional path can never match a real write, but the strict fix would belong in the freeze grammar (refusing the words outright), which would invalidate existing frozen plans.

- **Adaptive halts now record exactly ONE cause marker (#743).** `write-halt --reason consent` no longer co-writes a vestigial `escalated_to_full: security` line beside `escalated_to_full: consent`.
