That second half recorded a hand-off to the retired `full` path and is genuinely dead: every live reader takes the *first* `escalated_to_full` line (both `repair-state.js`'s `field()` and the adaptive-node reader use a non-global `/m` regex), the removed `stateMarkers`/`planMarkers` arrays were write-only, and its sole value-consumer — the autopilot `security_halt` stop reason — no longer exists anywhere in the tree. All four halt reasons now share one marker-splice path, replacing the `consent` special case (net −123 lines). `clear-halt` deliberately stays a superset and still strips the legacy `security` half, so a halt written by an older runtime clears instead of wedging on resume; that legacy tolerance is now covered by its own test rather than left implicit.

- **The reverse-containment gate is tightened as a consequence of the `gap-sweep` parse fix (#726).** A hand-typed `## Run gaps` row whose sample contained `)` previously never parsed, so `gapEntries` came back empty and the both-sides-empty vacuous pass fired — an unseeded observed gap slipped through finalization silently. Such a row now parses and is correctly refused `observed_gap_unseeded`, closing a real hole in the reverse-containment check rather than only a parsing papercut.

- **`gap-sweep` now diagnoses malformed `## Run gaps` mapping lines (#726).** The previously silent `continue` emits an advisory stderr warning naming the offending line, scoped to lines that look like a mapping attempt (a parenthesised sample followed by a `filed:`/`noise:` tail marker). Free-text bullets remain silently ignored by design; the warning never changes the parse result, the exit code, or the `--json` payload on stdout. Verified across all 91 `## Run gaps` sections in the repository: 113 parsed rows, zero parse differences, zero false-positive warnings.

- **The per-node close no longer computes gate-verify / verdict-check results nothing reads (#744).** `plan-validator --node-end` called `verifyGateExecution` and `verifyVerdictBlock` on every node close, and `commit-node` requested both on each of its two per-node arms, but the aggregator never read either. They were provably unread rather than merely unused-looking: per-node `overallOk` is `barrierPass && selectorPass`, `refuseReason` is derived only when `!overallOk`, and `barrierFailed` / `selectorFailed` are the exact logical complements of those two flags — so the `gate_failed` / `verdict_failed` branches are unreachable from every shipped CLI entry point. The computation is removed at its source and both call sites stop requesting it; the fused envelope pins both keys `null`. **No verdict moves:** across 20 differential scenarios the decision tuple (exit code, result, `overallOk`, reason, `barrierCheck.reason`, `outOfAllow`, `selectorCheck`) is byte-identical before and after, with the informational payload becoming `null` as the only field-level divergence. The standalone whole-plan blocking `--gate-verify` / `--verdict-check` subcommands are untouched and still refuse `gate_unsatisfied` / `verdict_not_pass`. A new whole-plan spawn-count lock guards against over-pruning. **No performance claim is made** — the four-subprocess-spawn cost this was filed against no longer exists since spawn fusion; the prune buys clarity. Mixed-version safe in both directions across the v6.24.0 installed runtimes. One accepted observability cost: a per-node barrier refusal envelope no longer carries the incidental unsatisfied-gate detail, which remains recoverable on demand via the standalone subcommands.

- **The point-of-use model-tier re-validation wall is pruned (#744).** `computeNextAction` re-validated every node's `model` cell against `NODE_MODEL_TIERS` before computing the ready-set, duplicating the `model_invalid` refusal the plan validator already applies at freeze. Measured across every `workflow-plan*.md` in the tree — 208 plans (163 `opus`, 270 `sonnet`, 232 `reasoning`, 150 `standard`): zero out-of-vocabulary tokens, so the wall has never fired in the recorded corpus. The prune's justification is scope rather than impossibility, and the code comment now says so explicitly instead of claiming such a cell "cannot enter a frozen plan" — measured otherwise: a plan whose `plan_hash` is hand-stamped via the exported `computePlanHash` (rather than produced by `--freeze`) carrying `model: haiku` on a non-reasoning-floor role yields `result: ok`, with the token emitted verbatim on both `nextNode` and `readyPending`. What bounds that is that the only silently-*corrupting* sub-case — a reasoning-floor role dropped below reasoning class — is refused on the dispatchable frontier for any non-reasoning-class model, in-vocabulary or not; any other bad token is a loud dispatch-time failure. **That guarantee did not initially hold, and the prune shipped only once it did.** Adversarial review caught that `enforceReasoningFloor` walked the READY frontier alone, while `speculativePending` is a second dispatchable frontier — `open-ready` hands it straight to dispatch, `adaptive-node` re-checks nothing — deliberately built to EXCLUDE ready nodes, so the floor check could never see it. Reproduced end-to-end: a `synthesizer` at `model: haiku` behind an open gate was refused `model_invalid` before the prune and opened for dispatch after it. The floor check now binds EVERY frontier the aggregator can emit, which also closes a hole that predates the prune entirely — an IN-vocabulary downgrade (a floor role at `standard`) was never caught on the speculative frontier by the pruned wall either, so this path ends up strictly stricter than before. Pinned by `FLOOR-5` with over-refusal controls, mutation-verified (removing the speculative check kills exactly the two intended assertions and leaves the controls green). The two register items decided KEEP are untouched and were confirmed present after the prune: the wait-budget / upgrade-compatibility wall and the reasoning-floor proof. `injectHash` is not exported, closing the other half of the historical dodge.

- **The `install-all.sh` coverage guard derives its installer set from the tree (#747).** `KNOWN_INSTALLERS` was hand-maintained and cross-checked in one direction only, so a newly added `install-*.sh` could be silently un-wired from the wrapper with every assertion still green. The guard now enumerates the tree and asserts both directions, so an unwired installer fails closed. The four-runtime execution-proof stub tests are unchanged.

### Removed

- **The Codex plan-run parent-equals-child inheritance proof is retired (#742).** The executor was instructed to spawn a throwaway probe before the first real node and compare parent and child session-JSONL `turn_context` model/effort, refusing `codex_profile_runtime_mismatch` on divergence. That re-proved at runtime a property already guaranteed structurally twice over: every generated Codex role profile omits `model` and `model_reasoning_effort` so a named child cannot fail to inherit, and the profile freshness gate mechanically enforces that omission on every entry and resume. The probe prose is removed from the routing skeleton and regenerated into the three Codex SKILL packs; the contract validators and route-reachability pins are INVERTED rather than deleted, so a reintroduced probe fails closed. The parent-session read survives for the reasoning-floor proof and the dispatch display fields, which are separate mechanisms.

### Known residuals
- **The reopen remedy for a false-blocked interior gate is deliberately NOT shipped (#745, second half).** When a false-block does occur, reopening the gate can still dead-end on `would_orphan_in_progress`, because the auto-opened terminal `finalize` sink is `in_progress` and is not a post-dominating gate role. The proposed remedy — auto-parking a "provably pristine" sink — was rejected as unsafe rather than deferred for convenience: the sink runs main-session-direct, and its first irreversible step (the feature commit) writes neither node evidence nor a sink-receipt journal, so no in-band marker can distinguish a pristine sink from one that has already committed, pushed, or merged. Parking the latter would double-apply irreversible work. Both orphan-guard twins (`reopen-node` and `repair-node`) therefore keep refusing; the only change in this release is tighten-only in the opposite direction (the new `would_strand_completed_dependent` refusal above), never a park or an exemption. The fix above removes the false-block that made the reopen necessary in the single-epoch case; a safe remedy needs an explicit sink-progress marker, tracked as follow-up work.
- **Cross-epoch interior-gate freshness is unchanged (#745).** When the seal attempt is absent from the review journal (cross-epoch rotation), interior-gate freshness still falls back to whole-candidate binding, so a false-block can still appear in child epochs. Acceptable tighten-only behavior until the cross-epoch journal work lands.
- **#745 stays OPEN.** Only the false-block half shipped; the reopen-remedy half above is deliberately unimplemented.
- **An ALREADY-wedged journal written by a pre-fix runtime is not auto-healed (#748).** The fix prevents the wedge; it does not rewrite history. All four runtimes installed at 6.24.0 carry the defect, so until this ships, avoid a mixed direct-repair-then-replay history at one gate. There is no clean in-place recovery from a journal that has already wedged — `repair-node`, `reopen-node`, `record-evidence`, the fused advance AND the replan escape all refuse — so the exits are preserve-base + discard + re-plan (the antichain-wedge recovery) or a manual journal edit. After this fix lands, a mixed history that was wedged mid-replay re-validates on the next read without intervention, because the relief is recomputed from the frozen graph rather than replayed from a stored decision.
- **The replay reuses the owner's ORIGINAL barrier baseline (#739 behavior, unchanged).** A descendant's already-written output therefore sits outside the reopened owner's declared write set at close and must be reverted through the sanctioned `revert-overflow` path. This is pre-existing #739 behavior, now covered by the new walkthrough scenario rather than changed by it.

## [6.24.0] - 2026-07-21

### Added

- **A review-gate failure owned by a non-maximal upstream writer now repairs IN-PLACE via a descendant replay instead of always escalating to a claim-preserving replan — closes #739.** Direct `repair-node` was admissible only when the selected writer was the unique graph-maximal producer of the gate's slice, so on any multi-writer plan (the shape the schema-2 common-certifier wall mandates) a finding owned by an upstream writer refused `repair_requires_replan` (`dependent_producer_replay_required`) and consumed one epoch of the ceiling-of-2. In three consecutive #725 phase runs *every* failed review wall escalated; a run one replan deep was then one failure from a hard dead end. Now, when the finding's unique semantic owner is a non-maximal completed writer whose descendant cone is mechanically replayable, `repair-node` performs the replay directly: it reopens the owner (seeded with the digest-bound repair brief), resets the owner's completed non-gate writer descendants (ledger rows + evidence + baselines) to pending, folds the post-dominating gates via the existing reopen machinery, and consumes the failed attempt — **zero epochs, `automatic_review_replans` untouched** — after which the cone re-runs and the gate re-reviews the whole (owner-modified) candidate as a closure attempt in the same scope lineage. The transaction is **crash-prefix idempotent** (the durable replay decision is written into the first repair mutation and the reset frontier lands atomically with the owner reopen; a crash at any failpoint resumes to the identical committed state) and **tighten-only**: the existing replan path is kept for every cone the replay cannot prove safe — an incomplete cone, a parallel-leg synthesis boundary in the cone, an in_progress non-gate descendant (orphan guard), ambiguous/cross-writer ownership, or a finding outside every writer's declared write set (true scope expansion). The anti-laundering journal-identity model is extended, not weakened: a replay records a proof-carrying `{owner, descendants}` marker that the identity validators (schema-1 and schema-2) **recompute and bound against the frozen graph on every read** — the descendant set must be a subset of the owner's structural non-gate-writer cone and the owner must uniquely own the attempt's own frozen findings, so the producer-proof exemption can never be pointed at an unrelated or already-certified producer, and a mid-replay journal (reset descendants at pending) re-validates instead of wedging. Replays count against `REVIEW_REPAIR_LIMIT`. Canonical `scripts/kaola-workflow-adaptive-node.js`; codex twin byte-copied; gitlab/gitea ports regenerated. New coverage in `scripts/test-adaptive-node.js`: the in-plan replay + on-read journal re-validation, crash-resume across every replay failpoint, the tighten-only refusals, and unit coverage of the structural-cone bound + forged-record rejection (anti-laundering surface).

- **`install-all.sh` — one entrypoint that reinstalls ALL FOUR runtime editions (Claude Code, opencode, Codex, Kimi) with a per-runtime PASS/FAIL summary, so no runtime is ever silently dropped again — closes #741.** The four installers were previously only tied together by operator memory, and Kimi was repeatedly missed on "reinstall the runtimes" passes (a partial reinstall looked identical to a complete one). The new top-level `install-all.sh` runs, in order, `install.sh` (Claude), `install-opencode.sh` (opencode), `install-codex-agent-profiles.js` (Codex), and `install-kimi.sh` (Kimi) — mapping each runtime to its own accepted flag set (the Claude installer takes `--forge`/`--profile` but no scope; the additive runtimes take `--global`/`--target`; the Codex installer takes neither `--yes` nor `--forge`) — and prints the installing short SHA plus a per-runtime PASS/FAIL table with each installer's captured exit code. It **never silently skips**: a non-zero installer exit is reported loudly (name + exit code + last output lines) and the wrapper **exits non-zero if ANY runtime failed** (continue-through by default so one failure never hides the other three; `--strict` fail-fast is opt-in). Flags: `--forge`/`--profile` (Claude only), `--global`/`--project[=DIR]` (the three additive runtimes), `--yes`, `--skip=<runtime[,...]>` (a LOGGED opt-out), `--check` (dry run — prints HEAD + the command each runtime would run, no mutation), `--help`. It is a **thin orchestrator, not a coupling**: it CALLS each installer unchanged and does NOT fold opencode/kimi into `install.sh`/`edition-sync.js`/`npm test`/the six routing surfaces (the D-530-02 additive-edition boundary is preserved). A new claude-chain contract test (`scripts/test-install-all.js`, 43 assertions) asserts `install-all.sh` references every known runtime installer from a single source-of-truth list — with a synthetic 5th-installer fixture proving the guard fails red — plus stub-driven behavioral coverage of the summary, non-zero-on-failure, `--strict`, `--skip`, and `--check` no-mutation semantics, so a future 5th runtime edition can never be silently omitted from the "install everything" path.

- **New persistent mutation spot-check gate proves the Phase E mega-test prune lost no real coverage — final phase of the adaptive-only consolidation, closing #725.** `scripts/test-mega-mutation-spotcheck.js` reintroduces 5 documented historical bug shapes — #611's delegation-outcome vocabulary bypass (`checkEvidenceShape`), an inverted `deriveGateMode`, an inverted `deriveGateEffect`, and a `reduceReviewReceipts` partitioned-refuted-swallowed defect (all three schema-2 review-engine, #693/#696/#697/#698), and #328's bundle `issue_numbers` order corruption — into isolated `fs.mkdtempSync` copies of `scripts/` (never the working tree, no network calls), and asserts each mutation is caught by exactly the pruned-suite assert the dedup map named as that invariant's sole keeper. Every case's probe is confirmed baseline-GREEN before the mutation is applied, and a demonstrated no-op mutation correctly reports ESCAPED, so a 5/5 CAUGHT result is a genuine catch, not a vacuous always-red harness. Run with `node scripts/test-mega-mutation-spotcheck.js` (~20s) — exit 0 means all 5/5 caught; it is a persistent, on-demand gate artifact, not wired into `npm test` or any `test:kaola-workflow:*` chain.

- **`run-chains.js` decomposes each chain into per-step timings, diff-scopes finalize's chain selection, and hoists duplicate steps across chains — Phase B of the adaptive-only consolidation (#725).** Every chain result now carries a `steps[]` array (`{command, duration_ms, exitCode}` per `&&`-joined step; a single-command/mocked chain still reduces to one step, so every pre-#725 mock-based test path is unchanged). In finalize context (`--project`/`--plan`, no `--chains`/`--mock-chain` override) the receipt's new `scope` block (`decision`, `reason`, `base`, `touchedEditionPaths`, `changedFileCount`) records an automatic chain-selection decision: a non-edition-touching diff runs the `claude` chain alone (`claude-only` / `non_edition_diff`), while an edition-touching diff or an unresolved diff base fails closed to all four (`all-four` / `edition_coupling` or `base_unresolved`); a pinned `--chains`, a mock run, or the bare/release path (no `--project`/`--plan`) keeps the pre-#725 unscoped behavior, so a release tag continues to require the full, unwaived four-chain receipt. A command duplicated across more than one selected chain now runs once in a shared `preamble` step (recorded in the receipt's own `preamble.steps[]`) instead of once per chain. The existing core-gated cross-chain concurrency (`KAOLA_RUN_CHAINS_CONCURRENCY`, per-chain `TMPDIR` isolation) is unchanged. Measured against the real chain suite (serial, clean host), the common case — a non-edition diff, claude-only — sees roughly a 20% wall-clock cut versus running all four; an edition-touching diff still runs all four (no cut), and the preamble hoist saves well under a second given the current shared-step set. Canonical `scripts/kaola-workflow-run-chains.js` edited; codex twin byte-copied; gitlab/gitea ports regenerated via the existing rename-normalized transform.

- **Mirror-before-dispatch — the plan-run loop now applies returned `taskTransitions` to the visible task list before dispatching the next role agent, not after — closes #718.** The task mirror is the operator's only live view of in-flight work; the node ledger stays the authoritative source of truth. The Band-1 mirror-before-dispatch line is added at the plan-run skeleton's step-3 dispatch seam (next to the existing never-dispatch-without-the-card discipline) and pinned durably via a new `required-blocks.js` manifest entry (`runtime_tag: both`, `surface_type_tag: both`), so `checkManifest` now obligates all six plan-run surfaces (the canonical command, its 2 forge command mirrors, and 3 SKILL packs) to carry it.

- **New additive runtime edition: Kimi Code CLI (`kimi` edition) — roles as Skills, inherit-only model tier, hooks as a managed `[[hooks]]` block.** A fourth agent runtime joins Claude Code, Codex, and opencode under the D-530-02 additive-edition boundary: canonical `agents/`/`commands/`/`hooks/` are untouched, and the edition is wired into neither `npm test`, `edition-sync.js`, `install.sh`, nor the six routing surfaces — parity is enforced by its own suite (`node scripts/test-kimi-edition.js`, 552 assertions). `scripts/sync-kimi-edition.js` generates the gitignored `.kimi/` tree: the 11 commands as directory-form Skills (so `/workflow-next` works as-is) and the 16 roles as `kaola-role-*` role-contract Skills, with every canonical dispatch card rewritten to Kimi's built-in subagents (read-only roles → `explore`, writers → `coder`, each prompt binding its `kaola-role-<role>` Skill). There is no Reasoning/Standard two-tier mapping — every subagent inherits the session model (the Codex inherit precedent); the planner tier survives as ledger metadata only, rendered by a new `kimi` key in `modelDisplay()` (adaptive-schema ×4 synchronized). Hooks ship as a generated `kimi-hooks.toml` fragment merged into the global Kimi `config.toml` as an idempotent managed block (validated with `kimi doctor config`, rollback on failure), with `SessionStart"compact"` mapped to `PostCompact`; smoke testing against kimi-code 0.26.0 caught Kimi's divergent hook payload field names (`tool_input.path` for Write|Edit, `agent_name` for SubagentStart), so the generator emits payload-adapted copies of the two affected hook scripts — verified live: the #607 gate-window fence denies an in-worktree write (exit 2) under a real headless Kimi session. Install with `./install-kimi.sh --global --yes` (project-level `--target` default; `--with-fast`/`--with-full` opt-ins partition the command set exactly like opencode). See [D-703-01](docs/decisions/D-703-01.md) and [docs/kimi-edition.md](docs/kimi-edition.md).

### Fixed

- **An interior schema-2 change-gate no longer goes falsely stale at `--verdict-check` when a disjoint downstream write moves the whole candidate — a correct, fully-certified plan finalizes without a reopen, closing #740.** `verifyVerdictBlock` → `checkSchema2One` (`kaola-workflow-plan-validator.js`) compared **every** change-gate's receipt `candidate_digest` to a single whole-tree `computeLandableTreeDigest`, so any downstream node that changed the candidate — even one whose write set is DISJOINT from the gate's surface (a CHANGELOG/docs node, a new test file) — declared every INTERIOR gate stale, even though only the final common certifier legitimately certifies the whole candidate. This is structural for the recommended shape (interior single-writer gates followed by more writers), and the sanctioned reopen remedy dead-ended (`would_orphan_in_progress` → `review_repair_delta_unavailable`), so a correct plan needed a destructive recovery or a manual gate bypass to finalize. Fix: an interior change-gate's freshness is now scoped to its OWN certified surface. For an adversarial-verifier change-gate that is NOT the plan's designated `code_certifier`/`security_certifier`, `checkSchema2One` resolves the gate's certified-producer write-set paths, reads the seal-time per-path blobs from the review journal's `candidate_declared`, and compares them byte-exactly (`<mode> <sha>`) against the current tree via a new `computeLandableBlobEntries` helper (`kaola-workflow-validation-runner.js`, same landable-index build as `computeLandableTreeDigest`); a gate whose surface is unchanged stays fresh regardless of unrelated downstream writes. The final common certifier keeps whole-candidate binding (it certifies everything last), and every shape the scoping cannot prove safe — the final certifier, a reviewer gate (post-dominance producers), no resolvable `certifies`, a missing seal attempt, or any git failure — fails **closed** to today's whole-candidate check (tighten-only; the stale reason string is unchanged, so the six-surface finalize propagation pin is untouched). A gate whose surface genuinely changed is still refused stale. New end-to-end regression `scripts/test-interior-gate-freshness.js` drives the real runtime to seal an interior adversarial change-gate + a disjoint downstream writer + a final certifier and pins both the fresh (P2) and stale-on-surface-change (P3) paths, plus `computeLandableBlobEntries` unit coverage. _Provenance: discovered on #725 Phase E finalize._
- **opencode reviewer profiles now resolve at runtime, unblocking every review-gated adaptive plan on opencode — #708.** `resolveReviewerProfileIdentity` probed `~/.config/opencode/kaola-workflow/agents/{role}.md` (a directory the installer never creates) while the actual profiles live at `~/.config/opencode/agent/{role}.md` (global) or `<project>/.opencode/agent/{role}.md` (project), AND the generated opencode profiles lacked the `resolved_profile_hash` field (the sync-opencode-edition transform discarded the schema-2 identity fields from the frontmatter). Result: every plan with a `code-reviewer` / `security-reviewer` / `adversarial-verifier` node hard-refused at `open-next` with `review_profile_unavailable` / `review_profile_identity_unavailable`. Fixes: `detectReviewRuntime` now recognizes the opencode install layout (`<config>/kaola-workflow/scripts/`); `reviewerProfilePath` probes the project, global, and self-dev candidate locations; `renderAgent` preserves `behavior_contract_version` / `behavior_contract_hash` for reviewer roles and re-stamps a fresh `resolved_profile_hash` over the transformed opencode bytes (the Claude hash no longer binds post-transform bytes).
- **The self-host TEST_CONSUMED_PATHS assumption no longer stales consumer-repo certifier receipts — #709.** `kaola-workflow-validation-runner.js` hardcoded `TEST_CONSUMED_PATHS = [README.md, CHANGELOG.md, docs/api.md, …]` — prose the four kaola-workflow test chains read — as in-band for the schema-2 candidate digest in EVERY repo. A consumer repo (iOS/Xcode, Makefile, etc.) has no such chains, so a finalize-sink CHANGELOG edit staled every certifier receipt. The plan-validator had the same leak via `SELF_HOST_TEST_CONSUMED`. Both modules now scope the list to self-host repos (a shared `detectSelfHostNpm` probes `package.json` for a `test:kaola-workflow:<edition>` script — the exact predicate `run-chains.js` uses). In a consumer repo, CHANGELOG/README/docs are validation-invisible (matching `isBarrierInvisible`), so a finalize-sink docs edit does not stale receipts. Memoized per repo-root; fail-closed (an indeterminate repo reads as self-host, the stricter band).
- **`--verdict-check` and `--finalize-check` now agree on the candidate band for the same tree — #710.** The candidate-band mismatch (validation-runner band vs plan-validator band) was a symptom of the #709 self-host leak: the two bands treated CHANGELOG/README/docs visibility differently, so one gate could pass while the other failed for the identical tree. The #709 shared `detectSelfHostNpm` — used by both `computeLandableTreeDigest` and `computeCodeTreeHash` — resolves the root cause: both bands agree on visibility in every repo kind, so a correct, fully-verified change whose only post-gate edits are validation-invisible docs/CHANGELOG can pass `--verdict-check` without a full plan re-run. The `--verdict-check` handler now passes the resolved git top-level `root` to `verifyVerdictBlock` so the probe addresses the correct tree.
- **A branchless / in-place adaptive run (`branch: TBD`) now completes the sink without a manual `git push` / `gh issue close` — #711.** A prior research session may pre-create the project folder (`claimed_at: N/A`, `branch: TBD`) and the adaptive planner's `owned` verdict preserves TBD, so the run commits straight to main (no feature branch, no worktree). `sink-merge --branch` hard-refused `TBD`, forcing a manual finish. The branchless path (valid with `--sink` only) treats the merge as a no-op (commit already on main), skips the worktree/branch teardown, and completes: push main + close issue + archive, recording `branch_mode: 'branchless'` in the closure receipt. `--branch TBD` without `--sink` still refuses (`branch_tbd_requires_sink`) — the legacy non-sink path requires a real feature branch.
- **Review-gated adaptive plans now resolve kimi-native reviewer profiles instead of hard-refusing `review_profile_unavailable` on every kimi install.** The kimi edition shipped reviewer roles as `kaola-role-*` Skills with `name`+`description` frontmatter only, so none of the three schema-2 identity fields existed where `resolveReviewerProfileIdentity` could read them, and `reviewerProfilePath` had no kimi candidate at all. Fixes: `sync-kimi-edition.js` emits a `<!-- kimi-reviewer-identity -->` body comment block for the reviewer gate roles — `behavior_contract_version` / `behavior_contract_hash` preserved from canonical plus a fresh `resolved_profile_hash` re-stamped over the final kimi bytes (the canonical Claude hash never binds post-transform bytes, the opencode renderAgent discipline); `reviewerProfilePath` gains a kimi branch probing project `<project>/.kimi-code/skills/kaola-role-<role>/SKILL.md` → global `<kimi-home>/skills/kaola-role-<role>/SKILL.md` → self-dev canonical; and `detectReviewRuntime` recognizes the `<kimi-home>/kaola-workflow/scripts/` install layout (realpath-compared against `$KIMI_CODE_HOME`/default `~/.kimi-code` — symlinked temp homes included — before the opencode pattern, so a stray `.opencode/agent/` profile can no longer hijack the kimi reviewer identity). Covered by new K6 positives + K9 hermetic end-to-end in `scripts/test-kimi-edition.js` (577 assertions).
- **Reviewer-profile runtime resolution and Codex preflight now agree with the installed layouts and the plan grammar — one bundle closing #712, #716, and #717 together.** #717: `detectReviewRuntime` (in `kaola-workflow-adaptive-node.js`, all four editions) only matched the SOURCE-TREE Codex plugin layout (`plugins/<name>/scripts/`); the INSTALLED marketplace cache inserts `<marketplace>` and `<version>` segments (`~/.codex/plugins/cache/<marketplace>/kaola-workflow[-gitlab|-gitea]/<version>/scripts/`), so detection fell through to the claude default and every schema-2 review-gate open hard-refused `review_profile_unavailable` on an installed Codex plugin. A tail-anchored, single-segment cache-tuple pattern now binds codex for all three forge editions; the explicit `KAOLA_WORKFLOW_RUNTIME` override still wins, kimi still detects before opencode, and unknown layouts keep failing closed to the claude default. #712: a plain claude install (`install.sh` SUPPORT_DIR `~/.claude/kaola-workflow/scripts/`) was swallowed by the #708 opencode pattern and routed down the opencode profile candidates; a claude branch now matches that layout BEFORE the opencode pattern, and `reviewerProfilePath`'s claude branch probes the previously-resolving `<support>/agents/<role>.md` first (self-dev canonical AND the documented `~/.claude/kaola-workflow/agents/` symlink workaround — already-linked installs keep resolving), then the native installed agent dir (`$KAOLA_AGENT_DIR` when set, else `~/.claude/agents` — the install.sh AGENTS_DIR convention), defaulting to the native candidate on a total miss so an absent profile still refuses `review_profile_unavailable` — never a silent wrong-runtime binding (resolver-side only; the installed claude profiles' self-hash stays valid, so no re-stamp or installer change). #716: `kaola-workflow-codex-preflight.js` treated every `## Nodes` role as delegated, so a valid frozen plan containing the built-in, intentionally non-delegable `main-session-gate` / `finalize` roles refused `role_not_in_template` (exit 3) and would have demanded profile files for them; the new `PLAN_BUILTIN_NON_DELEGABLE_ROLES` constant exempts exactly those two built-ins at the three availability-check sites (the `role_not_in_template` filter, the required-role union, and `checkProfiles`) while an unknown delegated role still refuses `role_not_in_template` and a missing delegated profile still refuses `profiles_missing`. The preflight fix is replicated byte-exact to the three plugin mirrors and the adaptive-node edition ports were regenerated via `node scripts/edition-sync.js --write` (parity green); regressions live in `scripts/test-adaptive-node.js` (2425 assertions), `scripts/simulate-workflow-walkthrough.js`, and `scripts/test-install-model-rendering.js`.
- **A repaired writer no longer wedges its folded downstream review gates, and close-node compliance rows are always appended validator-clean — one bundle closing #713 and #714 together.** #713: when `repair-node` folds a downstream review gate whose latest journal attempt is a settled PASS, it now durably records a fold-boundary marker on that attempt — `fold: { repair_attempt_id, selected_writer, candidate_digest, candidate_declared }` (the folding repair's attempt id and selected writer plus the sealed pass candidate; contract-2 attempts only; in the review journal, never in the purged `.cache/<gate>.md`; recomputed idempotently on every resume). `deriveRepairDelta` accepts the folded-pass lineage as a legitimate boundary alongside the ordinary settled/consumed FAIL and synthesizes the delta from the marker — fail-closed cross-checked against the attempt's own sealed partition, so a disagreeing marker refuses as tampering — and at the proven fold boundary an unchanged re-presented frontier counts as convergence, so the gate re-certifies instead of wedging on `review_repair_delta_unavailable`. The one remaining wedge, a marker-less sealed pass (a journal written by a pre-fix repair-node), still refuses `review_repair_delta_unavailable`, now with a detail naming the sanctioned recovery: release-and-adopt (claim release, then a fresh adopt-candidate claim) or a replan prepare from a `repair_requires_replan` refusal — documented in [the repair-node card](docs/plan-run-cards/reopen-complete-node.md). #714: `addCloseCompliance` now ALWAYS emits the canonical `role (node-id)` cell on the append path (already-emitted legacy bare rows still match and advance in place; no path emits a bare cell anymore), and `spliceComplianceSection` normalizes the append boundary so table rows stay contiguous with exactly one blank line before the next heading — `validateRequiredAgentCompliance` deliberately NOT relaxed. Regression pins live in `scripts/test-adaptive-node.js` (2479 assertions), including the issue's exact six-step wedge E2E over the real CLI in a real git repo.
- **A discarded project's archive is now committed by `release` itself, and an interrupted sibling sink's receipt no longer blocks a clean sink as foreign dirt — one bundle closing both halves of #715.** (a) `release` (and the `watch-pr`/`watch-mr` CLOSED sweep) archived the live folder into `kaola-workflow/archive/<project>.discarded-<ts>/` but never committed it, so the next `--sink` preflight refused the uncommitted discard archive as bucket-3 foreign dirt (`sink_blocked`) until an operator committed it by hand. A new shared `commitDiscardArchive` helper (canonical `kaola-workflow-claim.js`, codex twin byte-identical, hand-mirrored into the GitLab/Gitea ports) stages the archive helper's ACTUAL `dest` (never a reconstructed plain path — the #700 collision-suffix lesson), skips the commit on a diff-quiet guard, commits `chore: discard archive <project>` scoped to the dest pathspec, and verifies the archive is a tree at HEAD — local git only, `KAOLA_WORKFLOW_OFFLINE` does not skip it; in `cmdRelease` the call sits AFTER the in-place branch restore so the commit lands on the restored base branch. The emitted JSON carries the outcome: `discard_archive_committed: true|false`, plus `discard_archive_commit_detail` on failure (release also pushes a loud `warnings[]` entry; the watch sweep records the same fields on its per-folder `cleanups[]` entry) — a failed commit never throws past the emit and never strands the release. (b) `sinkPreflight`'s #518 receipt exemption was keyed on THIS sink's project, so an interrupted SIBLING sink's `kaola-workflow/{,archive/}<sibling>/.cache/sink-receipt.json` was classified bucket-3 foreign dirt and refused an unrelated clean `--sink`. The exemption now matches the EXACT path — exactly one `<project>` segment, live or archived, any project, unconditional across porcelain statuses — classification-only (this sink never stages, touches, or mutates the sibling receipt); look-alikes (`sink-receipt.json.tmp`, a nested `x/.cache/sink-receipt.json`, trailing-slash forms) and `sink-fallback.json` deliberately stay foreign dirt. Operator guidance for the in-progress-receipt vs terminal-stray distinction now lives in `docs/api.md` § Sink journal disposal at terminal success and `docs/workflow-state-contract.md` § Terminal journal disposal.
- **The discard-archive commit can no longer bind to the wrong branch, and an off-base `watch-pr`/`watch-mr` sweep now truthfully reports its skipped commit — follow-up repair to #715.** Two wrong-branch holes shipped with the #715 discard-archive commit: (a) on a NATIVE=0 feature-branch release with a dirty tree, the in-place base restore was skipped (the just-produced untracked `.discarded-` dest counted as dirt against the restore gate), so the archive commit landed on the DISCARDED feature branch; the restore gate now exempts ONLY the archive's actual `result.dest` (segment-boundary exact match), the base checkout is restored first, and the commit lands on the surviving base branch — with `commitDiscardArchive` itself resolving the current branch from the dest's own toplevel and refusing to stage on any non-base branch (defense in depth at both call sites). (b) A `watch-pr`/`watch-mr` CLOSED sweep running on a non-base checkout committed the archive onto whatever unrelated branch was current and reported `discard_archive_committed: true` with no branch disclosure; the sweep now reads `base_branch` BEFORE the archive move and skips the commit off-base — both ref tips untouched, the archive recoverable on disk — truthfully reporting `discard_archive_committed: false`. Both emit sites (the release top level and the sweep's per-folder `cleanups[]` entry) carry a new `discard_archive_branch` field on success AND skip, disclosing which branch received — or did not receive — the commit; the #715 semantics (diff-quiet guard, pathspec-scoped staging of the actual dest, never-throw emit, `KAOLA_WORKFLOW_OFFLINE` never skips) are unchanged. Canonical `kaola-workflow-claim.js` + codex twin byte-identical; GitLab/Gitea ports hand-mirrored.
- **The discard-archive commit's base-branch guard now validates that the recorded base is a real surviving branch — and re-verifies the commit against it after landing — instead of trusting `base_branch`, downgrading truthfully on any violation; guard-hardening follow-up repair to #715.** The #715 base binding trusted the recorded `base_branch` value itself, so a detached-HEAD sentinel or a falsified `base_branch` was honored as the commit target. `commitDiscardArchive` now refuses every such base BEFORE staging: `HEAD` (what `rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD` returns with no branch checked out) is rejected outright — it is by definition not a branch; the base must name a real local branch (`git rev-parse --verify refs/heads/<base>`, argument-array, never shell interpolation); a base naming the branch being discarded is refused (release passes the feature branch it knows it is discarding, the sweep passes the folder's own lane); and at the `watch-pr`/`watch-mr` sweep posture the base must equal the repo's default branch — the sweep has no restore step, so only the default branch is provably surviving-and-integration and a falsified base naming the current arbitrary lane is refused (the release's restored base carries no default constraint because the restore itself established it). AFTER the commit, the checkout is re-resolved (`rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD`) and must still equal the guarded base, and the HEAD commit must be reachable from it (`git merge-base --is-ancestor HEAD base`); any violation downgrades the emit to `discard_archive_committed: false` with `discard_archive_branch` disclosing the ACTUAL receiving branch (never the stale pre-race base) plus `discard_archive_commit_detail`, leaving the off-base commit and archive as recoverable residue. Every refusal/downgrade is truthful — never a claimed-but-unlanded `true` — never throws past the emit, and never strands the release or the sweep; the honest on-base commit path, diff-quiet guard, pathspec-scoped staging of the actual dest, and `KAOLA_WORKFLOW_OFFLINE` semantics are unchanged. Canonical `kaola-workflow-claim.js` + codex twin byte-identical; GitLab/Gitea ports hand-mirrored.
- **`detectReviewRuntime` no longer misclassifies a self-dev `kaola-workflow` checkout as an opencode install — closes #736.** A checkout literally named `kaola-workflow/scripts` with a sibling `package.json` also named `kaola-workflow` (e.g. a default-named clone of this repo) fell through every existing branch and matched the opencode tail-pattern, misrouting reviewer-profile resolution and runtime detection to opencode. A new guard — checking the same self-dev predicate `kaola_script()` already uses (`package.json` name === `'kaola-workflow'`) — now runs before the opencode tail-pattern and returns `claude` for exactly that layout; it is unreachable for any genuine opencode, kimi, claude, or codex install, since each of those layouts is captured by an earlier, more specific branch. Mirrored byte-identically across all four adaptive-node editions (root, Codex, GitLab, Gitea), with a new hermetic companion test in `test-adaptive-node.js` that seeds a fixture checkout named `kaola-workflow` and proves the guard red-to-green while every existing runtime-detection assertion stays green.
- **Follow-up repair to Phase C/D of the adaptive-only consolidation (#725): the kimi and opencode additive editions now match the hook retirement, and two latent Phase D prompt-diet regressions are fixed.** Phase C's `pre-commit`/`write-lane` hook retirement reached the four core editions but missed the two additive ones: `scripts/sync-kimi-edition.js` still tried to byte-copy both deleted canonical scripts (a hard `ENOENT` crash on every `--write`) and still actively wired `write-lane` into the generated `kimi-hooks.toml` (a load-bearing #607 gate-fence hook whose backing script no longer exists); it and `scripts/test-kimi-edition.js` now retire both, leaving only the `SubagentStart`/`PostCompact` `[[hooks]]` pair (`docs/kimi-edition.md` updated to match). `templates/opencode/plugins/kaola-workflow-hooks.js` still referenced the deleted `pre-commit` script in its `HOOK` map and its `tool.execute.before` `bash` branch — removing only the map entry (as Phase C did for the core editions) would have thrown a `TypeError` inside `path.join(..., undefined)` on every bash tool call, so the dead dispatch branch is removed too (`write-lane` stays, unaffected — it was already dormant there); `scripts/test-opencode-edition.js` and `docs/opencode-edition.md` updated to match. Separately, the Phase D prompt-diet's adapt/finalize trim collapsed each command's stray "MUST pass `model=`" dispatch note into its `## Agent Model Badge` section, so kimi's badge-strip transform (`sync-kimi-edition.js`) started deleting the only surviving occurrence wholesale instead of rewriting it — the strip now inserts the kimi inherit-model sentence in the badge's place — and `scripts/test-install-model-rendering.js`'s blank-markdown-line pin, which asserted on the now-trimmed `## Validation Delegation Policy` heading, is re-pointed to the surviving `## Steps` heading in `commands/kaola-workflow-finalize.md`.

### Removed

- **Overlap-band test prune removes 13 duplicate asserts from the mega-test files, and the bundle-claim entrypoint consolidates to a single keeper — Phase E (mega-test prune) of the adaptive-only consolidation (#725).** A dedup map measured the same-invariant overlap band between `scripts/test-adaptive-node.js` and `scripts/simulate-workflow-walkthrough.js`/`scripts/test-bundle-state.js` at 32 asserts across 3 clusters, with 13 prunable duplicate copies (~40%, well above the ≥25% target). 8 duplicate asserts are removed from `simulate-workflow-walkthrough.js` (the #611 AC5 `checkEvidenceShape` delegation-outcome block and the review-v2 R6 corpus-conformance header; 0 removed from `test-adaptive-node.js`, which keeps its fault-injection and fixture-corpus coverage as the sole altitude for both invariants), and 5 `issue_numbers[]`/`bundle_id`/`closure_policy` VALUE re-asserts are removed from `test-bundle-state.js`'s `testBundleStateParsing()` (0 removed from `test-claim-hardening.js`) — those VALUE checks now live solely at `test-bundle-claim.js` (the bundle-claim entrypoint's sole keeper) plus the walkthrough's bundle E2E journey. Post-prune band: 32→19 (a ~40% reduction). Every removal was content-matched against its cited keeper coverage before deletion, and the active-folders reader-shape asserts, the review-v2 R5 cross-edition block, and the classifier/orient `bundle_state_incoherent` blocks were left untouched as distinct subjects with no other coverage. Phase E closes #725 — the adaptive-only consolidation epic (retirement, run-chains diet, guard dedup, prompt diet, and this final mega-test prune) is now complete.

- **Prompt diet + validator narrowing trims the routing skeletons, the finalize/adapt commands, and the planner/contractor agent profiles to their machine-pinned floors — Phase D of the adaptive-only consolidation (#725).** Applying the three-band rule (Band 1 cycle-critical prose stays a terse bullet, Band 2 restating a JS gate's typed refusal cuts to a one-line pointer, Band 3 advisory narration cuts) across the prompt surfaces: `templates/routing/next.skeleton.md` 1138→894 lines and `templates/routing/plan-run.skeleton.md` 785→766 lines (regenerated into their 12 downstream next/plan-run command+SKILL surfaces via `generate-routing-surfaces.js --write`, `--check` byte-green); `commands/kaola-workflow-finalize.md` 1065→453 lines across its 2 forge command mirrors and 3 SKILL packs (661→621 lines each); `commands/kaola-workflow-adapt.md` 304→211 lines across its 2 forge mirrors; `agents/workflow-planner.md` 600→249 lines; `agents/contractor.md` 353→208 lines; and 109 lines of pure narration cut from 10 of the 11 hand-maintained role-agent profiles (1336→1227 lines total — `agents/knowledge-lookup.md` had no safe cut and stayed untouched). Each writer narrowed its own pins in the same node: the token pins fossilizing the trimmed prose across the five contract validators (the root/codex byte-twin plus the gitlab/gitea ports), the route-reachability block manifest, and the opencode/kimi mirror pins now assert only the surviving load-bearing tokens. The dormant `fast-compliance-backstop` pin chain — a legacy PIN block on the six finalize surfaces, its `required-blocks.js` manifest entry, the route-reachability SUPERSET-PROOF pair, and the opencode A20 mirror assert — is removed outright now that no runtime path reads it, and two fossil `assertBefore(..., '### Co-active Folders Advisory', '## Routing')` ordering pins (an artifact of a since-restructured `workflow-next` SKILL pack) are dropped from the gitlab/gitea contract validators. Targets beyond the recorded floors carry a certified one-line miss reason (pinned mandatory apparatus, e.g. the finalize command's byte-frozen reviewer-contract block and the contractor's guard-executed Step-8a block; and a structural command-vs-skill duplication in the routing skeletons that needs a slot refactor beyond a prose diet — see the node evidence for the full per-surface table); the three reviewer profiles (`code-reviewer`, `adversarial-verifier`, `security-reviewer`) are excluded outright — they are generator-owned behavior contracts rendered by `generate-reviewer-profiles.js`, and hand-trimming them would be generator drift. No provenance token (issue refs, decision IDs) entered any prompt surface. This phase does not close #725 itself — Phase E (mega-test prune) remains — but closes #718 and #736 (see above).

- **The `kaola-workflow-pre-commit.sh` and `kaola-workflow-write-lane.sh` advisory hooks are removed, and two redundant guard checks are deduplicated — Phase C (guard dedup) of the adaptive-only consolidation (#725).** The two hooks are deleted across all four editions (`hooks/`, `plugins/kaola-workflow{,-gitlab,-gitea}/hooks/`), along with their `PreToolUse` entries in all six `hooks.json` files, their `SUPPORT_HOOKS` entries in the install manifest (root + Codex twin), an `install.sh` sweep for lingering copies from a prior install, and every four-chain assertion that named them (the contract validators, `validate-script-sync.js`'s byte-copy groups, the four edition walkthroughs' hook tests, and `test-install-model-rendering.js`'s expected id set); the surviving `compact-context` and `subagent-dispatch-log` hooks are untouched. The `KAOLA_LANE_CONTAINMENT` and `KAOLA_GATE_WINDOW_FENCE` flags these hooks read remain defined in `kaola-workflow-adaptive-schema.js` but are now read by no runtime consumer — cleanup of that dormant machinery is out of this phase's scope. Separately, `edition-sync.js --check` drops its redundant re-check of `COMMON_SCRIPTS`/`BYTE_IDENTICAL_GROUPS` mirrors (`validate-script-sync.js` is the sole owner of that check; the unique 12-aggregator-port parity check is unchanged), and `kaola-workflow-adaptive-node.js`'s guard-prologue Layer-1 integrity check now recomputes the frozen plan's `plan_hash` in-process (via the plan-validator's `computePlanHash`) and skips the per-mutation `--resume-check` subprocess spawn on a match, falling back to the full `--resume-check` on any mismatch or error — a tampered plan still refuses `plan_integrity_failed` before mutation.

- **The `fast` and `full` workflow paths, and the install-time opt-in axis that gated them, are retired — Phase A of the adaptive-only consolidation (#725).** Adaptive is now the ONLY workflow path across every edition, not merely the unconditional default among three. `WORKFLOW_PATHS` collapses from `['fast','full','adaptive']` to `['adaptive']` (`kaola-workflow-adaptive-schema.js` ×4); `resolveInstalledPaths()` and `INSTALLED_PATHS_FIELD` are removed. 56 tracked files are deleted: the 4 canonical fast/full/phase4 transaction scripts + their 9 forge-port mirrors + 4 dead tests, 18 retired commands (`kaola-workflow-{fast,phase1..5}.md` × 3 editions), 18 retired Codex skill packs (`kaola-workflow-{fast,research,ideation,plan,execute,review}/SKILL.md` × 3 editions), and 3 fast/full-dedicated historical investigation docs. `kaola-workflow-claim.js` (×4): the scaffold's `workflow_path` default flips from `|| 'full'` to adaptive, and `cmdFinalize`'s plan-absent branch — which used to shell the now-deleted `*-full-advance.js phase5-verify` as an N/A pass — collapses to an unconditional typed refusal (`finalize_gate_unverified` / `adaptive_plan_missing`) before any archive/close side effect. `install.sh` drops `--with-fast`/`--with-full` (now generic unknown-argument errors) and never writes `installed_paths`; `install-codex-agent-profiles.js` (×3) drops the matching argv parsing and UNION writer. The 6 routing `next` surfaces, the 6 finalize commands, all validator/contract scripts, the forge test scripts, `package.json`'s test chains, and all six walkthrough simulators are updated to the retired tree (finalize fixtures that used to short-circuit via a `workflow_path: fast` marker now seed a real, gate-passing adaptive plan). The additive `opencode`/`kimi` runtime editions are re-synced and their own suites narrowed to the surviving surface (opencode 396 assertions, was 525; kimi 440 assertions, was 577). A stale `installed_paths` field from a pre-retirement install, or a stale `workflow_path: fast`/`full` on an old project, is tolerated on read (the classifier's tolerant parse simply never fires) but never re-written or newly honored — a non-adaptive path request is refused with the pre-existing typed `path_not_installed`, never a silent fallback. Superseding ADR: [D-725-01](docs/decisions/D-725-01.md) (supersedes [D-538-01](docs/decisions/D-538-01.md), [D-543-01](docs/decisions/D-543-01.md)). **Known residual, not closed by this phase:** the 3 Codex `kaola-workflow-finalize/SKILL.md` packs still shell the now-deleted `*-full-advance.js` at their phase5-verify point-of-use block, `commands/workflow-init.md` + `kaola-workflow-init/SKILL.md` (× 3 editions each) still document the retired `--with-fast`/`--with-full` install flags, and `install-opencode.sh`/`install-kimi.sh`/`.env.example` still reference the retired opt-in partition — tracked as open follow-up work, not fixed here.

## [6.23.1] - 2026-07-17

### Fixed

- **Codex now accepts every generated reviewer role instead of ignoring three malformed TOMLs at startup.** `code-reviewer`, `adversarial-verifier`, and `security-reviewer` previously emitted `behavior_contract_version`, `behavior_contract_hash`, and `resolved_profile_hash` as unsupported custom-agent top-level fields, so Codex discarded each role and could print the warning twice during one startup. The generator now emits only the supported role fields (`name`, `description`, `nickname_candidates`, and `developer_instructions`), keeps the runtime-neutral behavior identity plus complete-profile self-hash inside `developer_instructions`, and preserves the existing review receipt/runtime identity contract. Installer and preflight validation now reject the retired top-level shape, non-canonical keys/tables, backslashes anywhere in managed TOML, non-LF bytes, and raw TOML control characters across all 16 roles. Runtime identity resolution also binds the requested role to one matching profile name and behavior-core role. Real Codex config loading is warning-free while Claude/Codex/OpenCode reviewer behavior-core parity remains intact.
- **Codex profile/config preflight now follows the persisted runtime selection boundary instead of trusting a fresh lower layer.** It overlays owned transport/posture fields from HOME through every trusted repository-root-to-cwd `.codex/config.toml`, retains winning-path provenance for unsafe fields, and requires global `[projects."<absolute-path>"] trust_level = "trusted"` before treating a project Kaola footprint as loadable; unknown/untrusted project overrides refuse as `project_trust_required` rather than being validated as though Codex used them. Valid single-line and multiline top-level `project_root_markers` arrays drive layer discovery with the same strict duplicate/type checks. It validates the managed block byte-for-byte against the bundled role registry (including exact `config_file`, description, nickname metadata, and no extras), and treats quoted, dotted, indented, inline, or table-form `agents` declarations that collide with a managed role outside the markers as unsafe. Global or project wildcard, exact, and nested Kaola collisions refuse as `autofix_unsafe`, while unrelated user roles remain supported and doctor-green. The common entry gate resolves exactly one enabled installed Kaola edition from `codex plugin list --json` and executes only that registry row's exact marketplace/name/version cached preflight; repository-local lookalikes, old lexically-first cache versions, ambiguous registry state, symlinked cache paths, and malformed/non-JSON results refuse as `profile_preflight_refused`. Persisted-layer proof deliberately excludes per-process Codex `--profile` and `-c` overrides.
- **The Codex full-path review/fix loop now matches Claude's bounded review semantics.** GitHub, GitLab, and Gitea Codex review skills require a strict nonempty Phase 4 task table with every task complete, resume from the first incomplete review action, require the generated `code-reviewer` role instead of finalizing through an implicit local fallback, make an explicit invoked-or-N/A security file-risk decision, delegate admitted non-trivial fixes, record narrow validation, and re-run the relevant quality/security reviewer after the newest fix. Phase 5 now mechanically requires exactly one code-reviewer, security-reviewer, and review-fix decision with canonical nonce-bound evidence; approval binds the exact legacy receipt to `review_summary: no_blocking_findings` and `review_attestation: full_review_completed`, followed by exactly one final column-zero `review_conclusion: <substantive prose>` whose text contains at least 24 Unicode letter/number characters and four word tokens. The entire durable body rejects control, format, Unicode line/paragraph separator, and default-ignorable code points; machine labels and finding gate keys reject recognized compatibility/confusable and single-Damerau-edit near-spoofs while ordinary Unicode prose remains non-authoritative; canonical finding tokens require lowercase ASCII keys, ASCII = delimiters, and non-whitespace values, and a noncanonical line carrying all three assignment-shaped gate keys or a finding-like label followed by all three alternating key/value pairs refuses. The attestation and conclusion are emitted only after the full review. Receipt rows remain the only mechanical outcome authority and strict canonical `finding:` rows—not open-ended natural-language regexes—remain the only mechanical finding authority; conclusion presence, position, and minimum shape are mechanical while its prose content remains orchestrator context. Malformed, indented, case-shifted, Unicode-obfuscated, duplicate, unknown-vocabulary, or in-scope/open/fix rows refuse at both finalize and point-of-use verification. Reviewer receipts must be newer than Phase 4 progress and every review-fix artifact. Unresolved CRITICAL/HIGH findings still block by orchestrator judgment, and three non-converging fix-and-re-review iterations stop for operator direction.
- **Install and full-path file boundaries now fail closed on unsafe paths.** The Codex profile installer refuses symlinked, escaping, or wrong-type profile/config/hook/manifest destinations as `install_target_unsafe` before its first target write. Existing hook state is preserved and installation refuses before mutation when valid JSON has a non-object root, hook/event container, matcher group, handler list/entry, invalid handler type/value, or both Windows-command aliases instead of silently normalizing or overwriting it. Codex's valid top-level hook `description` metadata survives the managed merge. Hook-file and stable-script transactions use exclusive randomized stages, collision-safe backups, and recorded inode ownership so rollback and cleanup never remove a foreign replacement. Full-path transactions refuse linked or escaping project, cache, state, progress, review, reviewer, and review-fix artifacts as `project_path_unsafe`, and re-check the authority immediately before transaction writes.

### Upgrade notes

- Release versions: Kaola-Workflow/Claude Code `6.23.1` and the three Codex plugin manifests
  `4.23.1` share the single root release tag.
- Re-synchronize the release checkout before upgrading, then reinstall each runtime that is used on the machine: run the matching `install.sh --forge=<github|gitlab|gitea>` for Claude Code, refresh and reinstall the selected Codex marketplace plugin plus its global role profiles, and run `install-opencode.sh --global` for opencode. Restart Claude Code and Codex after their reinstall; re-run Codex `/hooks` only when changed hook content needs renewed trust.

## [6.23.0] - 2026-07-17

### Fixed

- **`reconcile-running-set` no longer drops ledger-complete writers' barrier baselines, which made a later post-dominating gate close refuse `writer_identity_unavailable` — #706.** A gate that has not yet recorded a review attempt captures every complete producer's writer identity (`barrier-base-*` file + `barrier-open-*` token + anchored ref) at its own close, so the #703 journal-referenced retention alone could not protect a pending gate's producers: both the orphan-baseline sweep (including the `no_running_set` path of a crashed serial run) and the #384 close-direction departing-member drop swept them, forcing a manual `--record-base` re-record against the current tree that lost the writer's historical diff attribution. Reconcile now retains every ledger-`complete` node's baseline on both paths — converging the close-crash repair to the same durable state a successful close leaves — while genuinely-orphaned baselines (discarded / never-completed nodes: ledger `pending` or no ledger row) still sweep, preserving the #680 crash-repair purpose and the stale-baseline-reuse protection. Regressions cover both consumer-reported repro shapes plus the still-sweeps negative (`scripts/test-replan.js`, `scripts/test-adaptive-node.js`); mirrored to all four editions.

### Added

- **Planner-owned re-plan epochs preserve the live claim and candidate instead of thawing or restarting a frozen adaptive run — #699.** Schema-2 epoch 1 now recognizes exactly two valid active-authority forms: planless (`active_plan_hash`, Planning Evidence hash, and first-node fields are `none`, with no plan/snapshot) and planned (the frozen plan hash and first-node tuple agree across plan, state, and task mirror); fresh claim writes planless and initial handoff atomically publishes planned. Offline mode overrides both native-worktree settings, creates neither a worktree nor an in-place branch, and anchors a no-history repository with the object-width all-zero commit plus its locally recomputed canonical empty-tree id. A real `repair_requires_replan` outcome is atomically persisted as a schema-2 `.cache/replan-source.json` envelope before return; `prepare` re-verifies that exact settled, unconsumed attempt, so no operator-authored bootstrap file is part of the product contract. `verifyCurrentEpochAuthority`
separately hash-verifies only the authored `Meta`/`Nodes`/`Node Briefs` surface while parsing and
consistency-checking the legally-progressing `Node Ledger`, `Required Agent Compliance`, and
task-mirror surfaces, closing a self-host defect where legal execution progress was refused as
authoring tamper; a claim-scoped `epoch_schema_version`/`epoch_lineage_id` envelope is checked
separately and typed-refuses when partially stripped or mismatched.
- **The epoch-2 child/snapshot and recovery proof is non-circular and exhaustive — #699.** Before planner dispatch, the transaction derives `snapshot_authority_projection` from immutable parent/source/entry-CAS authority; the schema-2 child field historically named `parent_snapshot_manifest_digest` binds that projection digest, while the later full manifest separately seals the projection, exact child and attestation, every file row, manifest self-digest, and exact manifest-file digest. Historical schema-1 `pending` children are accepted only through the generic `legacy_external_binding` path when all external seals agree. The re-plan implementation inventories 41 durable-write label families plus deterministic per-file/per-seam labels, and its focused suite exercises 12 CAS mutations (prepare, pre-freeze, pre-snapshot, pre-activation × candidate, claim root, inherited frontier) with zero unintended epoch/count/dispatch/snapshot effect. The genuine Case-B route starts only when review authority is absent and requires a completed parent, four exact typed `diagnosis_complete` artifacts, artifact-only writers, and exact child citations; its zero-cost exemption remains one-shot. Activation replaces the complete Planning Evidence tuple and refuses stale first-node/hash/task-mirror state. Every finalize/release/merged-watch/closed-watch archive caller proceeds only after `archiveSucceeded(result)` accepts `archived:true` or idempotent `skipped:"source-missing"`; all other results stop before cleanup or remote disposition. See [D-699-01](docs/decisions/D-699-01.md).
- **Verification status for the #699 candidate remains PARTIAL, not certified green.** The versioned-authority engine (transaction rotation, the four CAS seams, snapshot/manifest sealing, and the current-epoch authority split) is implemented; its focused re-plan suite, resume, edition/script sync, and forge forbidden-token checks pass. Lifecycle/publication callers (archive, finalize, release, watch cleanup, and the walkthrough's nested subprocess-timeout handling) and packaged-edition fixture parity (Codex/GitLab/Gitea snapshot-authority fixtures) are separate, still-in-progress write surfaces. Integrated PASS is withheld until the named code and security certifiers and the three read-only falsification nodes (budget exhaustion, history/archive integrity, and publication/edition parity) each record a pass; see the project's Node Ledger and per-node gate evidence for current status.
- **Reviewer profiles now render from one versioned behavior contract — #696.** `code-reviewer` and `adversarial-verifier` are generated deterministically for Claude and all three Codex plugin editions from strict behavior data plus closed runtime adapters. Every render carries a shared normalized behavior hash and a complete-byte resolved profile hash; OpenCode preserves the same normalized core. Generation and installation prove filesystem identity, not identical stochastic reviewer output or proprietary runtime prompt loading. See [D-696-01](docs/decisions/D-696-01.md).
- **`security-reviewer` now renders from the versioned behavior contract instead of a hand-maintained embedded block — #704.** Its behavior contract (version 2, approval domain outcomes `approved`/`changes_requested`) joins `templates/reviewers/behavior-contracts.json`, and `scripts/generate-reviewer-profiles.js --write`/`--check` is the sole writer for its five Claude outputs and three Codex TOML editions — closing the drift class where a hand-edited copy silently diverged from the canonical contract. Behavioral content (OWASP Top 10 coverage, the high-risk pattern list, secret and false-positive controls, `fix_role=security` remediation routing, and the machine verdict/findings emission) is preserved; the previously conflicting Codex profile description is unified to the runtime-neutral behavior description, and severity now guides urgency while the admitted in-scope classification decides gate effect (matching the schema-2 review reduction the runtime already applies to this role).
- **Schema-2 validation uses a deterministic local runner — #697.** Newly authored plans refine the existing hash-covered `validation_command` with bounded cwd, repetition, all-pass, timeout, and environment-allowlist fields. The local runner scrubs the environment, binds command/candidate/executable/toolchain identities, records secret-safe digests, and reduces repetitions to `pass`, `fail`, or `inconclusive`. Verified legacy plans retain D-547-01's reader-only command and one-run compatibility mapping with no runner semantics or byte rewrite. See [D-697-01](docs/decisions/D-697-01.md).
- **Candidate-bound reviewer contract 2 adds immutable findings, declared reducers, and a common-certifier wall — #698.** New plans carry explicit contract/version, gate claim/surface/aggregation, certifier, and inherited-frontier metadata. Runtime-neutral contexts, runtime-specific profile binding, canonical receipts, stable anchor-derived finding UIDs, strict closure progress, validation-vector inheritance, deterministic `sequence`/`replicated_majority`/`partitioned_all` reduction, and G4 freshness now fail closed. `review_scope_expanded` and `review_nonconvergent` produce a typed `replan_required` handoff; replacement-epoch activation remains outside this change. See [D-698-01](docs/decisions/D-698-01.md).

### Fixed

- **Worktree-postured sinks no longer archive an evidence-empty `.cache/` — the worktree's per-node evidence lands in the archive, and an evidence-empty archive of a run that recorded evidence is now a loud typed refusal instead of a silent `status:sinked` — #707.** A consumer-repo closure audit found every bundle-run archive of a two-day window with an EMPTY `.cache/` (node evidence unrecoverable) despite the #676 `verifyArchiveComplete` gate. Two compounding causes: (1) the `--sink` merge step staged the worktree's `kaola-workflow/<project>/` before removal but landed it all-or-nothing — only when main's live folder was absent — and on every worktree-postured run the branch/planner-era main copy exists, so the stage (holding the run's untracked `.cache/<node-id>.md` evidence, which the barrier exempts from commits) was always discarded; (2) the sink's sole-archiver finalize then archived main's evidence-empty live folder through `archiveProjectDir`'s in-place `renameSync` branch, which never calls `verifyArchiveComplete` at all — and even the copy-path check is SOURCE-relative, so a faithful copy of an already-gutted source passes trivially. Fixes: the merge step now union-lands the staged worktree copy per FILE (checkout-resolved branch-tracked content still wins; sink journals never land), so the archive carries the run's real evidence and the existing #700 `archive_commit` makes it durable at HEAD — squash can no longer orphan it; `archiveProjectDir` gains an opt-in `requireNodeEvidence` floor (passed by the sink) that refuses `node_evidence_missing` — before any mutation, on BOTH archive branches — when the `## Node Ledger`'s `complete` rows prove evidence was recorded but the source folder no longer holds it (the ledger lives in `workflow-plan.md`, so it survives a gutted `.cache/`; every close is evidence-checked, so `complete` ⇒ recorded); `verifyArchiveComplete` gains the matching opt-in `requireLedgerEvidence` hardening that adds the ledger-proven evidence to the required set so an evidence-empty archive of a completed run can never pass; and the sink's finalize step now surfaces any evidence-losing archive refusal (`node_evidence_missing`, the #676 lossy-copy family — both name the lost files in `missing`) as a typed `sink_incomplete`/`finalize` refusal with exit 1, leaving the finalize step NOT done (resumable after the operator restores the evidence) and the live folder untouched, instead of silently swallowing it and reporting `status:sinked` while the closure ran. Plan-less folders (legacy fast/full, discards, minimal fixtures) and the benign journal-only-live-dir resume shape are unaffected. Hand-ported to the GitLab/Gitea editions with per-edition sink regressions; new claude-suite regressions cover the worktree evidence landing, the loud refusal + recovery re-run, and the `verifyArchiveComplete` hardening.
- **`repair_requires_replan` now has a first-class resumable path, and `reconcile-running-set` no longer deletes the baselines that path needs — #703.** A post-dominating gate that fails on a finding spanning multiple upstream writers (no unique maximal routable producer) is diagnosed by #701 (`repair_writer_ownership_mismatch` / `dependent_producer_replay_required`) and resolved by the #699 planner-owned re-plan epoch: the planner authors a child that inserts a repair-writer upstream of the failed gate, and the activated child imports the parent journal through the digest-bound `legacy_import` pointer, marking the failed source attempt consumed so `open-next` resumes (opening the inserted writer) instead of the in-place `--freeze --repair` path's circular `review_attempt_unresolved` block — with no hand-editing of `review-attempts.json`. This bundle closes the two remaining gaps that made the in-place path unrecoverable: (1) **baseline retention** — `reconcile-running-set`'s orphan-baseline sweep now KEEPS the barrier baseline of every COMPLETE producer referenced by an unresolved (`consumed_by: null`) review attempt's `producer_bindings` (the `.cache/barrier-base-<writer>` files that are `repair-node`'s non-discard recovery refs); a genuinely-orphaned baseline still sweeps, so a mid-diagnosis `reconcile-running-set` (even a `no_running_set` one) no longer deletes the exact files the repair needs; and (2) **packaging** — `install.sh` now ships `docs/plan-run-cards/*.md` into `$SUPPORT_DIR/docs/plan-run-cards/` (fail-closed on a missing source or the canonical `repair-routing.md`), so operators can follow the barrier/repair/resume recovery recipes the plan-run command cites instead of navigating the refusals blind. The `repair-routing.md` card now documents the multi-writer-finding recovery and the reconcile retention guarantee, and a real end-to-end integration regression (`test-replan.js`) drives the committed child epoch through `open-next` to prove the resume.
- **Investigation adversarial verifiers now close as analytical results instead of deadlocking repair — #693.** One forward-reachability classifier supplies the investigation/change-gate mode at every lifecycle seam. Complete investigation outcomes (`refuted`, `not_refuted`, or `indeterminate`) close with no gate effect and no product-repair journal, while malformed or stale execution still retries fail-closed; change gates continue to pass only on `not_refuted`. Execution status, domain outcome, and gate effect are now separate harness contracts. See [D-693-01](docs/decisions/D-693-01.md).
- **Sink-merge keep-open and cross-run resume are now correctly scoped to the CURRENT claim — a reused project name no longer replays a stale receipt's steps, and a keep-open run can no longer report a clean sink over a silently-retired issue — #694.** `readCurrentClaimTs` reads the current run's `## Sink` `claim_ts:` from `workflow-state.md` (the branch ref first, then the live/plain-archive/every collision-suffixed-archive working-tree state) and stamps it — alongside a new `keep_open_requested` boolean recording THIS run's `--keep-issue-open` intent — into every fresh `sink-receipt.json` build. At load time, a receipt whose recorded `claim_ts` (or `started_at`, for a pre-#694 shape) predates the current claim's timestamp is reinitialized rather than resumed: its recorded steps, including a prior `closure: done`, are never replayed under the new run's flags, closing the gap where the SAME project name, reclaimed by a later run, silently inherited an earlier run's receipt state. A same-cycle keep-open flag flip against an already-`done` closure step now also re-evaluates closure live instead of replaying the stale decision. A new terminal `keep_open_verify` guard then runs on every path to success regardless of which steps were skipped: whenever keep-open is in force (the live flag, the receipt's `keep_open_requested`, or the archived `issue_action: comment_keep_open`) and the forge still reports the issue closed after this guard's own backstop reopen attempt (for a stale/resumed receipt where the #517 `push_main` reopen never ran), the sink refuses `sink_incomplete` at step `keep_open_verify` instead of reporting `status:sinked` over a kept-open epic that was actually auto-closed and never recovered.
- **Sink archive disposal now targets the ACTUAL archive destination, including a collision-suffixed one, instead of a hardcoded plain path — #700.** `archiveProjectDir` may choose a collision-suffixed `kaola-workflow/archive/<project>.archived-<ts>/` destination when the plain `archive/<project>/` already exists; the sink's `finalize` step now records that exact destination as `archive_dest` on the receipt, and every downstream consumer reads it instead of assuming the plain path: `archive_commit` stages/commits the real destination directory (previously `git add` of the hardcoded plain path staged nothing for a suffixed archive, so the diff-quiet guard skipped the commit while `stepDone` still ran unconditionally — silently losing the archive, roadmap-source removal, and regenerated `ROADMAP.md`), and `disposeSinkJournals` now also sweeps the recorded `archive_dest`'s `.cache/` journals plus every other `<project>.archived-*` folder's journals, not just the four plain-path candidates. When a `--sink` run is the SOLE archiver (no prior `cmdFinalize --keep-worktree` already wrote terminal metadata), `persistSinkClosureMetadata` now also writes the same `## Closure` and `## Attestation` blocks `cmdFinalize` writes into the archive dest — using a real dispatch-log attestation probe, never a fabricated one — so the archive is never left without terminal metadata.
- **A keep-open `--sink` run that is the SOLE archiver now RETAINS the kept-open issue's roadmap source instead of dropping the open issue from the mirror — #705.** #694 verified the keep-open END-STATE on the forge, but the roadmap-source removal sat adjacent to — not inside — that guard: when the sink owned the last archive (no prior `cmdFinalize` passed `keepRoadmapSource`), its `finalize` step called `archiveProjectDir` with no keep-open signal, so `reconcileRoadmapForClosure` removed `kaola-workflow/.roadmap/issue-N.md` for an issue that stays OPEN and regenerated a `ROADMAP.md` that no longer listed it. The sink's `finalize` step now derives keep-open intent with the same three-source derivation the terminal `keep_open_verify` guard uses (the live `--keep-issue-open` flag, the receipt's `keep_open_requested`, or the archived/live `issue_action: comment_keep_open`) — factored into one shared `deriveSinkKeepOpen` helper now reused by the finalize step, `persistSinkClosureMetadata`, and the terminal guard — and passes the kept-open member set to `archiveProjectDir` as a new per-member `excludeIssues` option. `reconcileRoadmapForClosure` retains exactly those members' roadmap sources (an open issue stays tracked in the mirror) while still removing every closing member's source, so a mixed close/keep-open bundle drops only the closing issues. A closing run passes an empty set and removes sources exactly as before.
- **`repair-node`'s route-candidate ownership resolution is fixed, and now blocks a tail-writer reopen that owns none of the open findings — #701.** The schema-2 route builder (`schema2RouteCandidates`) was passing the finding's `compat` OBJECT to `resolveOwningNodes`, which expects a path string; the object stringified to `"[object Object]"`, matched no node's declared write set, and every schema-2 route therefore carried `ownership_candidates: []` unconditionally — the semantic owner of a finding was structurally unrecoverable. It now resolves ownership from the finding's immutable `primary_anchor.path` instead (an anchor-less finding, e.g. an `evidence_observation`, still resolves to `[]` and degrades to the pre-existing generic `repair_requires_replan`, never a false ownership accusation). With real ownership now resolvable, the repair-reopen admissibility check gains two refusal cases layered on the existing #682/#684 graph-maximal proof: `repair_writer_ownership_mismatch` when the requested writer IS graph-maximal but owns NONE of the attempt's still-open blocking findings (a serial tail writer that cannot actually repair the flagged code — previously this would have silently proceeded on the graph-maximal proof alone), and `repair_requires_replan` with `reason: dependent_producer_replay_required` when the requested writer is NOT graph-maximal but IS the unique semantic owner, and has completed non-gate writer descendants (`completedNonGateWriterDescendants`) whose outputs a safe reopen would have to replay (a case that previously dead-ended as the bare, owner-less `repair_requires_replan`). Both refusals name the resolved owner/descendants so the planner-owned re-plan can re-derive from the correct node.
- **Leg-dispatched role agents now receive an absolute evidence path inside their own leg worktree — #692.** `deriveDispatchChannel` resolves the dispatch card's `upstream_evidence` entries through `resolveEvidenceCachePath` when the open provisions a leg (`openIntoLeg`), so a role agent working from a leg cwd reads the parent worktree's upstream evidence at its real absolute location instead of a cwd-relative path that resolved inside the leg (where the file does not exist). Serial (legless) opens are byte-identical to before.
